title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
Quick Debrief

Welcome back, everyone. Before we dive into new material, let’s debrief the homework. I asked you to log into the Meridian app, ask ten business questions, and bring your best answer and your worst answer. I want to hear from three or four of you.
This debrief should take about 10 minutes. The goal is to surface the friction that sets up today’s topic. Most of the “worst answers” will involve questions that span multiple systems — that is exactly where we are headed.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
Homework Takeaways



What Worked


Simple lookups were fast and accurate
Single-system aggregations felt effortless
Charts and narratives generated on the fly




What Broke


Questions spanning multiple systems
Customer names that don’t match across CRMs
Joining data with no common key




The homework was designed to surface exactly this friction. Today we confront it directly.


Let me summarize what I expect you found. On the “what worked” side: simple lookups were fast and accurate, single-system aggregations felt effortless, and the charts and narratives that the agent generated on the fly were genuinely useful. That is the promise of Session 1 delivered.
But on the “what broke” side — and this is the important part — questions that spanned multiple systems hit a wall. If you asked “How does revenue compare to headcount growth by division?” the agent had trouble because revenue lives in the CRMs and headcount lives in Workday. Customer names that appear differently across CRMs — “General Electric” in one system and “GE Industrial Solutions” in another — made cross-system questions unreliable. And joining data with no common key was simply impossible for single-system queries.
The homework was designed to surface exactly this friction. If single-system queries are the easy win, cross-system queries are the hard problem — and it is the problem most enterprises actually face. Today we confront it directly.
Ask the room: who had a question that broke because it needed data from more than one system? Let two or three people share their specific examples.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
The Cross-System Problem

Now let’s name the problem we are solving today. This is the cross-system problem, and it is the central challenge of enterprise data.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text: Your data isn’t missing.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
It’s everywhere.

Scattered across 10 systems that were never designed to talk to each other.


Your data is not missing. Nobody deleted it. Nobody forgot to collect it. It is everywhere — scattered across ten systems that were never designed to talk to each other. Salesforce knows about Industrial division customers. The Legacy CRM knows about Energy division customers. HubSpot knows about Safety. Workday knows about employees. NetSuite knows about finances. Zendesk knows about support tickets. Each system is doing its job perfectly well in isolation. The problem is that the most valuable business questions require data from more than one of them.
This is not a Meridian Corp problem. This is an every-company problem. If your company has been around more than a few years, you have this exact situation — maybe with different system names, but the same fundamental challenge.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
Today’s Learning Objectives


By the end of this session, you will be able to:


Explain why enterprise data is scattered and why consolidation costs millions
Watch AI query multiple systems and merge results in real time
Map your own company’s data landscape and identify cross-system blind spots



Here are our learning objectives for today. By the end of this session, you will understand why enterprise data is scattered and why traditional consolidation costs millions. You will watch an AI agent query multiple systems and merge the results in real time — something that would normally take weeks of engineering work. And you will map your own company’s data landscape and identify the cross-system blind spots where valuable questions go unanswered.
Let’s start with a demo that picks up right where Session 1’s email chain left off.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
Demo: The Cross-System Merge

Now let’s see the cross-system merge in action. This is the centerpiece demo of today’s session. Open the Meridian app now. We are going to ask the exact question from Session 1’s email chain — the one that the BI team said would take three weeks.
This demo segment should take about 20 minutes total, covering two cross-system queries.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
“Which customers buy from multiple divisions?”



1


Query Salesforce


Pull Industrial division customers and revenue




2


Query Legacy CRM


Pull Energy division customers and revenue




3


Query HubSpot


Pull Safety division customers and revenue




4


Fuzzy Merge


Python matches names across all three systems




The agent writes and executes SQL against each system, then uses Python with fuzzy string matching to reconcile customer names. No warehouse. No ETL pipeline. No 3-week wait.


Open the Meridian app and type: “Which customers buy from multiple divisions?” Watch what happens. Narrate each step as the agent works through it.
Step one: the agent queries Salesforce to pull Industrial division customers and their revenue. Step two: it queries the Legacy CRM for Energy division customers. Step three: it queries HubSpot for Safety division customers. And here is the critical part — step four: it uses Python with fuzzy string matching to reconcile customer names across all three systems. “General Electric” in Salesforce, “GE Industrial Solutions” in the Legacy CRM, “GE Safety Division” in HubSpot — the agent figures out these are the same company and merges the data.
No data warehouse. No ETL pipeline. No three-week wait. The agent queried three separate systems, pulled the data into Python, matched customer names using fuzzy logic, and gave us a unified answer. Point out the SQL the agent generated for each system — it is visible in the response. Point out the matching logic. This transparency is important because it lets you verify the answer.
Now, I want to be honest about what just happened. The fuzzy matching is not perfect. It catches about 80 percent of cases. Some matches might be wrong — is “Honeywell Safety Products” the same entity as “Honeywell International”? That is a business judgment call. We will talk more about this kind of friction later in the session.
If the Meridian app is down or slow, switch to the pre-recorded fallback demo and narrate over it.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
section: Demo: Revenue Per Employee by Division
text:
Demo: Revenue Per Employee by Division



Data Sources


CRMs (Salesforce, Legacy CRM, HubSpot) — revenue by division
Workday — headcount by department and division




What the Agent Does


Queries revenue from each CRM
Queries headcount from Workday
Merges on division, calculates revenue/employee
Returns a table and a bar chart




Two questions. Four systems. Both answered in under a minute. But how does this actually work?


Now let’s ask a second cross-system question. Type into the Meridian app: “What is the revenue per employee by division?” This one is interesting because it requires data from two completely different kinds of systems.
On the left, we need revenue data, which lives in the CRMs — Salesforce, the Legacy CRM, and HubSpot. On the right, we need headcount data, which lives in Workday. The agent queries revenue from each CRM, queries headcount from Workday, merges on division, calculates revenue per employee, and returns a table and a bar chart.
Two questions. Four systems. Both answered in under a minute. Compare that to what it would take to get these answers through traditional channels at most organizations. Someone would need to pull reports from each system, manually reconcile the data in a spreadsheet, and deliver it — probably in a few days if it is a priority, longer if it is not.
Now, the question you should be asking is: how does this actually work? What is happening behind the scenes? We will get deep into that in Session 3. But for today, I want to focus on the business implications and the data integration landscape.
The reason I am showing you these two demos is that they represent the most common cross-system question patterns in enterprises: customer overlap across business units, and efficiency metrics that combine financial and operational data.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
How Data Moves Today

Now let’s step back and understand the landscape. What you just saw — an AI agent querying multiple systems and merging results on the fly — is one approach to data integration. But it is not the only one, and it is not always the right one. Let me walk you through how data moves in enterprises today so you can understand where this new approach fits.
This lecture segment should take about 12 minutes.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
Four Patterns of Data Integration



APIs


Real-time, point-to-point
Each connection hand-coded
Breaks when vendors change endpoints




iPaaS


MuleSoft, Workato, Boomi
Pre-built connectors
Still needs mapping and maintenance




ETL / Warehouse


Extract, transform, load into Snowflake/BigQuery
Single source of truth
$500K–$5M and 6–18 months




CSV Export


The “just email me the file” approach
No governance, no audit trail
Surprisingly common




There are four main patterns for how data moves between systems in enterprises today. First, APIs — real-time, point-to-point connections. Each one is hand-coded, and they break when vendors change their endpoints. Second, integration platforms like MuleSoft, Workato, or Boomi — these offer pre-built connectors, which is better, but you still need to map fields and maintain the connections over time.
Third, the ETL and data warehouse approach — extract, transform, load into something like Snowflake or BigQuery. This gives you a single source of truth, which is the gold standard. But the cost is 500K to 5 million dollars and 6 to 18 months to build. And fourth — and I hesitate to even call this a “pattern” — the CSV export. The “just email me the file” approach. No governance, no audit trail. And it is surprisingly common. If you are honest with yourself, how much of your company’s cross-system data integration happens in Excel spreadsheets that get emailed around?
Ask the room: which of these four patterns does your company rely on most? I bet the answer is some combination of all four, depending on which systems and which teams are involved.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
The Traditional Answer



$500K–$5M


Cost of a data warehouse build




6–18 mo


Timeline to production




60–70%


Of data warehouse projects that stall or underdeliver (industry estimates)




The warehouse is the gold standard — but the cost, timeline, and failure rate are why most mid-market companies don’t have one. The alternative: query each system where it lives and merge on the fly.


Let these numbers land. 500K to 5 million dollars to build a data warehouse. Six to eighteen months before it is in production. And 60 to 70 percent of data warehouse projects stall or underdeliver — that is an industry estimate, not something I am making up.
The data warehouse is the gold standard for a reason. It gives you a single schema, a single source of truth, strong governance, and fast queries because the data is pre-modeled. But the cost, the timeline, and the failure rate are exactly why most mid-market companies do not have one. If you are a 500-million-dollar distributor like Meridian — or even a billion-dollar company — spending 5 million and 18 months on a warehouse that has a better-than-coin-flip chance of stalling is a hard sell to the board.
The alternative is what you just saw: query each system where it lives and merge on the fly. No warehouse. No ETL. No 18-month timeline. But there are real tradeoffs, and I want to be honest about them.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
Two Approaches, Real Tradeoffs





Data Warehouse
AI Agent (Query-in-Place)




Setup cost
$500K–$5M
Low (API credentials + schema docs)


Time to first answer
6–18 months
Days to weeks


Data freshness
Batch (hourly/daily)
Real-time queries


Governance
Strong (single schema)
Weaker (distributed)


Complex joins
Fast (pre-modeled)
Slower (runtime merge)


Scalability
Excellent
Limited by API rate limits


Best for
Regulated, high-volume
Exploratory, ad hoc


Audit trail
Built-in
Requires custom logging





This is not either/or. Most companies will use both — warehouses for governance-critical reporting, agents for the long tail of questions that never justified a dashboard.


Take a minute to read through this comparison table. The data warehouse wins on governance, complex joins, scalability, and audit trail. It is the right answer for regulated reporting, for metrics you run every day, for anything that goes to the board or to a regulator. The AI agent wins on setup cost, time to first answer, and data freshness. It queries systems in real time — no batch lag.
The key insight is at the bottom of the slide: this is not either/or. Most companies will use both. Warehouses for governance-critical reporting — the numbers that go in your 10-K, the metrics your regulators audit. And AI agents for the long tail of questions that never justified building a dashboard or a data pipeline. The ad hoc questions. The “I wonder if…” questions. The CEO’s Monday morning question that the BI team says will take three weeks.
Think of the agent as the complement to the warehouse, not the replacement. The warehouse handles the 20 percent of questions that account for 80 percent of regulatory and operational importance. The agent handles the other 80 percent of questions that nobody was asking because the cost was too high.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
Cross-System Friction

Now let’s look at the unsexy but critical part of cross-system data: the friction. This is what makes merging hard, whether you are building a warehouse or using an AI agent. The technology changes; the friction does not.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
Why Merging Is Hard










Problem
System A
System B
Impact




Naming conventions
customerName
customer_name
Schema mismatch


Date formats
2025-03-15
March 15, 2025
Parse failures


Customer identity
General Electric
GE Industrial Solutions
No common key


Customer identity
Honeywell International
Honeywell Safety Products
Wrong match


ID systems
SF-001234
ACCT-5678
No shared identifier





Meridian has ~25 customers that appear under different names across its three CRMs. This is not a bug — it is the normal state of enterprise data.


Let me walk through the specific problems that make cross-system merging hard. Look at this table. Naming conventions: one system uses camelCase for column names, another uses snake_case. Same data, different labels — the agent has to know that “customerName” and “customer_name” refer to the same thing.
Date formats: one system stores dates as “2025-03-15” and another stores them as “March 15, 2025” — or even worse, as a text string in MM/DD/YYYY format. If you are doing date-based aggregations, this causes parse failures and wrong results.
But the hardest problem is customer identity. “General Electric” in Salesforce and “GE Industrial Solutions” in the Legacy CRM — these are the same company, but there is no common key linking them. No shared customer ID. Just a name that is spelled differently in every system. And it gets worse: “Honeywell International” and “Honeywell Safety Products” — are those the same entity? Or is one a subsidiary? That is a business judgment call, not a technical one.
Meridian has about 25 customers that appear under different names across its three CRMs. This is not a bug in our simulation. This is the normal state of enterprise data. Every company I have worked with has this problem. If you have grown through acquisition or have divisions with autonomy over their CRM, you have this problem.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
The Fuzzy Matching Problem


How Does the Agent Resolve “General Electric” vs. “GE Industrial Solutions”?


Exact match fails — the strings are completely different
Fuzzy matching (Levenshtein distance, token-set ratio) catches ~80% of cases
Semantic matching (embeddings) catches more, but is slower and less transparent
Human review is still needed for ambiguous cases (is “Honeywell Safety Products” a subsidiary or a division?)



This is the unsexy core of data integration. No amount of AI eliminates the need to decide what your entities are and maintain a mapping.


So how does the agent resolve “General Electric” versus “GE Industrial Solutions”? Exact string matching fails completely — the strings are totally different. Fuzzy matching — techniques like Levenshtein distance and token-set ratio — catches about 80 percent of cases. It is looking for strings that are similar enough to be plausible matches. Semantic matching using embeddings can catch more, but it is slower and less transparent. And for ambiguous cases, you still need human review.
Here is the honest truth, and I want to underline the text at the bottom of this slide: this is the unsexy core of data integration. No amount of AI eliminates the need to decide what your entities are and maintain a mapping. Someone in your organization needs to define: is “Honeywell Safety Products” the same as “Honeywell International” for reporting purposes? That is a business decision. The AI can suggest matches, but a human needs to confirm them and maintain the mapping over time.
This is often where the excitement about AI data tools meets the reality of enterprise data management. The technology is impressive, but the hard work is still defining your master data. Keep this in mind as we move into the hands-on portion.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
Hands-On: Cross-System Queries

All right, time to get hands-on again. This time, you are going to tackle cross-system queries — the hard stuff. We are going to start with a timed challenge to add a little competitive energy, and then open it up for exploration.
This hands-on segment should take about 16 minutes total.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
Timed Challenge


The Question

“Which division’s top customer is most at risk of churning?”




1


Identify


Find each division’s #1 customer by revenue (CRMs)




2


Assess Risk


Check support ticket volume and sentiment (Zendesk)




3


Merge


Combine revenue + support data, rank by churn risk




4


Recommend


Draft a 3-sentence executive summary




You have 15 minutes. This requires querying at least two systems and merging the results. Pay attention to how the agent handles name matching.


Here is your timed challenge. The question is: “Which division’s top customer is most at risk of churning?” This is a genuinely hard question because it requires data from at least two systems.
Let me walk through the steps. First, you need to identify each division’s number one customer by revenue — that requires querying the three CRMs. Second, you need to assess churn risk — and for that, check support ticket volume and sentiment in Zendesk. Third, you need to merge the revenue data with the support data. And fourth, draft a three-sentence executive summary with your recommendation.
You have 15 minutes. Pay close attention to how the agent handles the name matching between the CRM data and the Zendesk data. Does it get the right customer? Does it flag any uncertainty? If the agent struggles, try breaking it into smaller steps — ask for the top customer by division first, then separately ask about their support tickets, and then ask the agent to combine the two.
I will give you a five-minute warning and a one-minute warning. If you finish early, try a different cross-system question of your own design. Drop interesting findings in the Zoom chat.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
Data Map Workshop

Now we are going to shift from Meridian’s data to your data. This workshop is one of the most important activities in the entire course, because what you produce here carries forward all the way to your capstone strategy in Session 8. Take this seriously — it is the foundation for everything that follows.
This workshop and the subsequent breakout should take about 25 minutes total.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
Map Your Company’s Data



Step 1: List Your Systems


Identify 3–5 core systems (CRM, ERP, HRIS, etc.)
What data lives in each?
Who owns it?




Step 2: Draw the Gaps


What cross-system questions can nobody answer easily?
Where do people resort to spreadsheets?
What decisions are made on gut feel because the data is too hard to get?




Step 3: Identify Quick Wins


Which two systems, if connected, would unlock the most value?
What’s the simplest question that currently takes weeks?




Spend 10 minutes sketching this out. We’ll share and compare.


I want you to spend the next 10 minutes sketching out your company’s data map. There are three steps.
Step one: list your core systems. Think about three to five systems your company runs on — your CRM, your ERP, your HRIS, your ticketing system, whatever they are. What data lives in each? Who owns it?
Step two: draw the gaps. What cross-system questions can nobody answer easily today? Where do people resort to spreadsheets to bridge the gap between systems? What decisions are being made on gut feel because the data is too hard to assemble?
Step three: identify the quick wins. If you could connect any two systems, which two would unlock the most value? What is the simplest question that currently takes weeks to answer?
You do not need a polished document — a sketch on paper or a few bullet points in a notes app is fine. The point is to start thinking concretely about your own data landscape. This map becomes the foundation for your capstone strategy, so take a few minutes to think it through carefully.
I will be available in the main room if you have questions. We will go to breakout rooms after this for comparison.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
What We’re Skipping (For Now)


The Pre-Work Nobody Talks About

Before any AI agent can query your real systems, someone must:

Catalog data — what tables exist, what do the columns mean?
Document schemas — data types, relationships, business rules
Provision API access — authentication, rate limits, permissions
Classify PII — which fields contain personal data? What are the retention rules?





60–90


Typical pre-work before an agent touches real data




5–10


People typically involved in data readiness




0


Vendors who mention this in their demos




Before we wrap up, I want to name something important that the demos do not show. Before any AI agent can query your real systems — not the Meridian simulation, but your actual production systems — someone has to do a significant amount of pre-work.
You need to catalog the data: what tables exist, what do the columns mean? You need to document the schemas: data types, relationships, business rules. You need to provision API access: authentication, rate limits, permissions. And you need to classify PII: which fields contain personal data, and what are the retention rules?
Look at these numbers. Sixty to ninety days of typical pre-work before an agent touches real data. Five to ten people typically involved in data readiness. And zero — zero — vendors who mention this in their demos. I am naming this now because I want to calibrate your expectations. The demos make this look easy — and the technology part genuinely is easier than it used to be. But the organizational pre-work is real, and no vendor will mention it in their sales pitch. When you evaluate vendors in Session 5, remember this slide.
This segment should take about 6 minutes.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
Before Next Session


Homework


Read the enterprise systems background document (provided)
Complete your data map: list at least 5 systems your company uses and the top 3 cross-system questions you can’t answer today



Monday’s session: How Does This Actually Work? — we look under the hood at the agent loop, the system prompt, and where the code runs.


Here is your homework for before Monday’s session. First, read the enterprise systems background document — it will be emailed to you. This gives you context on how enterprise systems are structured and why they end up the way they do.
Second, and this is the more important one: complete your data map. Take the sketch you started today and expand it. List at least five systems your company uses and identify the top three cross-system questions you cannot answer today. Be specific. “Our CRM and ERP don’t talk to each other” is a start, but “We cannot answer what our customer acquisition cost is by segment because marketing spend is in HubSpot and revenue is in NetSuite” is what I am looking for.
This data map is not just homework — it is the foundation for your capstone strategy. The more specific you are now, the better your capstone will be.
On Monday, we look under the hood. You have spent two sessions as the user. Now we are going to answer the question: how does this actually work? We will look at the agent loop, the system prompt, and where the code runs. See you then.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
Key Takeaway

Let me leave you with the key takeaway from today.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text: The hardest part isn’t technology.

title: Session 2: Our Data Is Everywhere
text:
It’s that your systems disagree.

They disagree on who your customers are, what your products are called, and when things happened. AI can bridge the gap — but someone still has to define the truth.


The hardest part of cross-system data is not the technology. It is that your systems disagree. They disagree on who your customers are — “General Electric” versus “GE Industrial Solutions” versus “GE Safety Division.” They disagree on what your products are called. They disagree on when things happened because of different date formats and different fiscal calendars.
AI can bridge the gap. You saw it today — fuzzy matching, cross-system merges, intelligent reconciliation. But someone still has to define the truth. Someone has to say: yes, these three names are the same customer. Someone has to maintain that mapping over time. Someone has to decide what “revenue” means when each system calculates it slightly differently.
That is the organizational work that makes the technology work. And it is why your data map homework matters — you are starting to identify where those disagreements exist at your company, which is the first step toward resolving them.
Thank you for a great session. See you Monday.

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
section: The Day AI Came for Software
text:
The Day AI Came for Software

“In February 2026, AI agents wiped $2 trillion from software company valuations in a single week. Investors began betting that AI could replace BI dashboards, workflow tools, reporting layers, and Tier 1 support.”


— The market signal, February 2026


Welcome, everyone. Let me start with a story that sets the stage for everything we are going to do over the next four weeks. In February 2026, AI agents wiped two trillion dollars from software company valuations in a single week. Two trillion. That is not a typo. Investors decided, almost overnight, that AI could replace large categories of enterprise software — BI dashboards, workflow tools, reporting layers, even Tier 1 support.
Now, whether or not you agree with the market’s verdict, the technology behind that bet is real, and it is what we are going to be working with for the next four weeks. This course teaches you to use it, understand how it works, evaluate its reliability, and build a strategy for deploying it at your organization.
Before we dive in, a quick reminder of what the pre-program video covered: we have the Meridian Corp simulation, which is a realistic enterprise data environment with ten systems, and for those who want to go deeper, the Linux VM for hands-on building. Today, we start as users. We will get our hands dirty with data right away. Any questions about the course arc or logistics before we begin?
This opening segment should take about 10-12 minutes. Keep the energy high — this is the hook for the entire course.

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
text:
The Market Signal



$2T


Market cap erased from software companies




70%


Of BI dashboards estimated to go unused




3 wks


Typical wait for a custom report at many organizations




The market is betting that conversational data access will replace the tools you buy today. What does that mean for your company?


Let these numbers sink in for a moment. Two trillion dollars in market cap erased from software companies. Seventy percent of BI dashboards estimated to go unused — that is not my number, that is industry research. And three weeks is a typical wait for a custom report at many organizations. Three weeks to answer a question your CEO has right now.
The market is making a very specific bet: that conversational data access — asking questions in plain English and getting instant answers — will replace the tools companies buy today. Dashboards, reporting layers, even some of the analyst headcount that supports them. Whether that bet is right or wrong, the technology is here, and it is what we are going to experience firsthand today. So the question for each of you is: what does this mean for your company? We will come back to that question throughout the session.

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
text:
A Familiar Story

Now let me tell you a story that I suspect will sound very familiar. This is the cold open — it is designed to be a little provocative. I want you to think about whether something like this has happened at your company.
This segment should take about 5 minutes including discussion.

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
text:
The Email Chain

Monday 9:14 AM — CEO to VP Sales: “Which customers buy from multiple divisions? I want the list by Wednesday.”


Monday 11:02 AM — VP Sales to BI Team: “Need a cross-division customer overlap report. CEO priority.”


Monday 3:30 PM — BI Team: “We can start next sprint. ETA: 3 weeks. We need to join Salesforce, Legacy CRM, and HubSpot — different schemas, no common key.”


Monday 3:45 PM — CEO: “Three weeks?”


Read through this email chain with the class. Monday morning, 9:14 AM — the CEO fires off a simple question: which of our customers buy from multiple divisions? Seems straightforward, right? By 11 AM it has landed on the BI team’s desk as a priority request. And by 3:30 PM, the BI team responds: three weeks. They need to join Salesforce, the Legacy CRM, and HubSpot — different schemas, no common key. The CEO’s reaction at 3:45 says it all: three weeks?
Now, ask the room: has this happened to you? Not this exact scenario, but the pattern — a senior leader asks what feels like a simple question, and the answer is weeks away because the data lives in different systems that were never designed to talk to each other. I want to hear a couple of examples. Let two or three people share briefly before moving on.
The reason I am showing you this is that it sets up the core promise of what we are about to see. What if this question did not take three weeks? What if it took thirty seconds?

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
text: What If It Took 30 Seconds?

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
text:
“Which customers buy from multiple divisions?”

Type the question. Get SQL, a chart, and a narrative — instantly.


This is the transition to the live demo. Open the Meridian Corp app now. Type exactly this question into the chat: “Which customers buy from multiple divisions?” Let the class watch as the agent processes the query, generates SQL, runs it against multiple systems, and returns an answer with a chart and narrative — all in about thirty seconds.
As the results come back, narrate what is happening: “Notice it is querying Salesforce for the Industrial division, the Legacy CRM for Energy, and HubSpot for Safety. It is pulling customer lists from each, then using Python to match names across systems and find the overlaps.” Ask a follow-up question the class suggests — something a dashboard could never handle because it was not pre-built for it.
This demo should take about 8 minutes. The goal is visceral impact — the class just heard about a three-week wait, and now they are watching the same question answered in real time. If the app is slow or down, switch to the pre-recorded fallback demo and narrate over it.

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
text:
Dashboard vs. Chat



Traditional Dashboard


Pre-built by analysts weeks ago
Fixed filters and dimensions
Can’t answer questions it wasn’t designed for
New question = new ticket = new sprint




AI Chat Interface


Ask any question in plain English
Agent writes SQL, runs it, returns results
Generates charts and narrative on the fly
Follow-up questions in seconds




Now let’s connect what you just saw back to the broader picture. On the left, you have the traditional dashboard — pre-built by analysts weeks ago, with fixed filters and fixed dimensions. It can only answer the questions it was designed for. If you have a new question, that is a new ticket, a new sprint, a new wait.
On the right, the AI chat interface. You ask any question in plain English. The agent writes SQL, runs it, returns results. It generates charts and narratives on the fly. And follow-up questions take seconds, not weeks. The key difference is not speed — it is flexibility. The dashboard answers the questions someone anticipated. The chat interface answers the questions you actually have, right now, in the moment.
I want to be clear: dashboards are not going away. They are still the right tool for operational monitoring — metrics you check every day. But for the long tail of ad hoc questions that never justified building a dashboard? That is where this technology changes everything.

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
text:
Today’s Learning Objectives


By the end of this session, you will be able to:


Explain why AI agents are disrupting the BI software market — and why it matters now
Experience conversational data access vs. traditional dashboards
Ask business questions in plain English and get instant answers with charts



Here are our learning objectives for today. By the end of this session, you will be able to explain why AI agents are disrupting the BI software market and why this matters right now — not in five years, right now. You will have experienced conversational data access firsthand, contrasted it with traditional dashboards, and you will have asked your own business questions in plain English and gotten instant answers with charts.
We are about to shift gears into the business implications of what you just saw, and then we are going to get hands-on with the Meridian app for the rest of the session. Let’s talk about what this means strategically.

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
text:
The Business Lens

Now let’s step back from the technology and put on our business hats. The technology you just saw — querying enterprise data in plain English, getting instant charts and narratives — is what the market believes will displace dashboards, reporting teams, and data integration tools. That is a big claim. Let’s think about what it actually means for your organizations.
This business lens segment should take about 10 minutes, including the discussion on the next slide.

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
text:
The Key Question

If anyone in your company could query data in plain English — no SQL, no tickets, no dashboards — what decisions get made faster? What questions finally get asked?


This is the key question I want you to sit with. If anyone in your company — not just the analysts, not just the people who know SQL — could query data in plain English, what changes? Think about it in two dimensions.
First, what decisions get made faster? The decisions that are already being made, but slowly, because someone has to pull the data, build the report, and route it through the right people. Those decisions get compressed from days or weeks to minutes.
But the second dimension is more interesting: what questions finally get asked? There are questions in every organization that nobody bothers asking because the cost of getting the answer is too high. It would take three weeks and a dedicated analyst, so people just go with their gut. When the cost of asking drops to zero, those questions start getting asked. And that is where the real value is.
Ask the room: can anyone give me an example of a question at your company that nobody asks because it is too hard to get the data? Let two or three people respond. This discussion is important — it makes the abstract concrete for each participant.

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
text:
Hands-On: Single-System Queries



1


Simple Lookup


“What are our top 10 customers by revenue?”




2


Filtering


“Show me open deals over $100K in the Energy division.”




3


Aggregation


“What is average deal size by sales rep this quarter?”




4


Analytical


“Which reps are trending above quota? Show me a chart.”




We’ll progress from simple lookups to analytical questions. Each prompt hits a single system — Salesforce pipeline, NetSuite financials, or Workday headcount.


Now we are going to get hands-on. For the next twenty minutes, you are going to work through a progression of queries against the Meridian app. We start simple and build up. Step one is a simple lookup — “What are our top 10 customers by revenue?” This is the equivalent of a basic database query. Step two adds a filter — “Show me open deals over 100K in the Energy division.” Step three introduces aggregation — “What is average deal size by sales rep this quarter?” And step four is analytical — “Which reps are trending above quota? Show me a chart.”
Each of these prompts hits a single system. We are not crossing systems yet — that comes in Session 2. The point here is to get comfortable with the conversational interface and to feel the difference between waiting for a report and having a conversation with your data.
If you get stuck or the app gives you something unexpected, that is fine — note it and keep going. We will debrief as a group afterward. This hands-on segment should take about 20 minutes.

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
text:
Meet Meridian Corp


Your Simulation Environment


Meridian Corp — a $500M industrial distributor
3 divisions: Industrial (~$250M), Energy (~$150M), Safety (~$100M)
10 enterprise systems — CRMs, ERP, HRIS, ticketing, and more
41 database tables across all systems
26,000+ rows of realistic, interconnected data spanning 3 years



Before you start querying, let me tell you about the company you are going to be working with. Meridian Corp is a fictional 500-million-dollar industrial distributor. It has three operating divisions: Industrial at about 250 million, Energy at about 150 million, and Safety at about 100 million. The company runs on ten enterprise systems — CRMs, ERP, HRIS, ticketing, and more — with 41 database tables and over 26,000 rows of realistic, interconnected data spanning three years.
This is not a toy dataset. It has the same kind of cross-system friction you deal with in real life: different naming conventions, different date formats, customers that appear under different names in different systems. We built it to feel real because the lessons only land if the data feels real.

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
text:
Meridian’s Systems at a Glance



Industrial Division (~$250M)


Salesforce CRM
NetSuite Finance
SAP Operations




Energy Division (~$150M)


Legacy CRM (custom-built)
QuickBooks Finance
Oracle SCM




Safety Division (~$100M)


HubSpot CRM
Shared NetSuite Finance




Corporate HQ


Workday HR (all divisions)
NetSuite Consolidation
Zendesk Support




Three divisions. Three CRMs. Ten enterprise systems. This is the cross-system reality most enterprises face.


Take a moment to look at this layout. Three divisions, each with their own CRM and financial systems. Industrial uses Salesforce and NetSuite. Energy uses a legacy custom-built CRM and QuickBooks. Safety uses HubSpot and shares NetSuite with Industrial. Then corporate has Workday for HR across all divisions, NetSuite for consolidation, and Zendesk for support.
Notice the pattern: three CRMs. Three different data models. Three different ways of storing customer information. This is not bad IT management — this is what happens when companies grow through acquisition, when divisions have autonomy, when different teams picked the best tool for their needs at different times. It is the normal state of enterprise data, and it is exactly why that CEO’s question about cross-division customers took three weeks.
For today, you will be querying individual systems. Next session, we tackle the hard problem of querying across them.

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
text:
Try It Yourself

All right, it is time to get your hands on the keyboard. Everyone should have the Meridian app open in your browser. If you have not logged in yet, do that now — the URL and credentials were in the pre-session email. I will give you a moment to get set up.
This is the transition into the hands-on portion. You will have about 30 minutes total — first with guided prompts, then open exploration. Let’s go.

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
text:
Guided Exploration


Sample Prompts to Try


“What was Meridian’s total revenue last year, broken down by division?”
“Who are the top 5 customers in the Industrial division by lifetime value?”
“Show me headcount by department. Are any teams growing faster than revenue?”
“What’s our average days-to-close for deals over $200K?”
“Plot monthly revenue trends for all three divisions on one chart.”



Start with the prompts above, then ask your own questions. The goal is to feel the difference between waiting for a report and having a conversation with your data.


Here are some sample prompts to get you started. You can type these in exactly as written or riff on them. “What was Meridian’s total revenue last year, broken down by division?” is a good warmup — it hits the financial systems. “Who are the top 5 customers in the Industrial division by lifetime value?” goes into the CRM. “Show me headcount by department — are any teams growing faster than revenue?” pulls from Workday and requires the agent to do some analysis. “What is our average days-to-close for deals over 200K?” is a more analytical CRM query. And “Plot monthly revenue trends for all three divisions on one chart” tests the charting capability.
Start with these, then branch out. Ask questions you would actually want to know about a company like this. The goal is not to test the app’s limits — we will do that in Session 6. The goal right now is to feel the difference between waiting for a report and having a conversation with your data. Notice how quickly you can iterate: ask a question, see the answer, ask a follow-up, refine.
I will be monitoring the chat and highlighting interesting discoveries as they happen. If you find something surprising, drop it in the Zoom chat so everyone can see.
This guided exploration should take about 12 minutes, then we will open it up for free exploration.

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
text:
Breakout Discussion



Reflect


What surprised you about the speed or quality of answers?
What question did you try that worked unexpectedly well?
What question stumped the agent — and why?




Apply


What would this look like at your company?
Who currently answers these questions — and how long does it take?
What decisions are delayed because the data isn’t accessible?




Time for breakout discussions. I am going to put you into groups of three or four. You will have about 15 minutes. There are two sets of questions to work through.
First, reflect on what you just experienced. What surprised you about the speed or quality of answers? What question did you try that worked unexpectedly well? And importantly, what question stumped the agent — and why did it fail? Understanding the failure modes is just as valuable as seeing the successes.
Second, apply it to your world. What would this look like at your company? Who currently answers these kinds of questions — is it a BI team, an analyst, someone in finance? How long does it take? And what decisions are being delayed or not made at all because the data is not accessible?
Each group should be ready to share one key insight when we come back together. I will give you a two-minute warning before we reconvene.
This breakout should take about 15 minutes. When groups report back, listen for patterns — if multiple groups mention the same pain point, highlight it.

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
text:
Before Next Session


Homework


Log into the Meridian app and ask 10 business questions against any single system
Write down one question the agent answered well and one where it was unsatisfying or wrong
Bring both to Session 2



Here is your homework for before our next session on Wednesday. I want you to log into the Meridian app and ask ten business questions against any single system. Ten is the minimum — ask more if you like. The point is to build fluency with conversational data access.
But here is the important part: write down one question the agent answered well — really nailed it — and one question where the answer was unsatisfying or outright wrong. Bring both to Session 2. I am going to ask three or four of you to share your best and worst answers, and those examples will set up exactly what we cover next time.
A hint: if you ask questions that span multiple systems — like comparing CRM data with financial data — you may start running into some interesting friction. That is by design. It is exactly what Session 2 is about.

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
text:
Key Takeaway

Let me leave you with the key takeaway from today’s session.

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
section: The bottleneck was never the data.
text: The bottleneck was never the data.

title: Session 1: Exploring Data with AI
section: It was access to the data.
text:
It was access to the data.

AI agents don’t replace your analysts — they give everyone else the ability to ask questions without waiting in line.


The bottleneck was never the data. Your company has plenty of data — in Salesforce, in NetSuite, in Workday, in a dozen other systems. The bottleneck was access to the data. It was the three-week wait for a custom report. It was the dashboard that could not answer the question you actually had. It was the analyst who was busy with someone else’s priority.
AI agents do not replace your analysts. Your analysts are still the people who understand the business context, who know what questions to ask, who can verify whether an answer makes sense. What AI agents do is give everyone else the ability to ask questions without waiting in line. That is the shift. The cost of asking a question drops to zero, and when that happens, the questions start flowing.
Thank you for today. Do your homework, play with the app, and come ready on Wednesday to talk about what happens when the data you need is not in one system — it is everywhere. See you then.

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
section: Last Week, You Were the User
text:
Last Week, You Were the User

This is the section divider that opens the bridge segment. Take a breath here and let the title land. You are transitioning the class from “user mode” — where they have spent the last week asking questions and getting answers from the Meridian app — into “builder mode,” where they will understand what is happening behind the scenes. This bridge segment should take about 10 minutes total, covering this slide and the next two.
Set the tone: “For the past week, you have been the user. You typed questions in plain English, you got charts and narratives back, and hopefully a few of you found something genuinely surprising in the data. Today we flip perspectives. We are going to look under the hood and understand how the technology actually works. By the end of this session, you will know exactly what happens between the moment you press Enter and the moment the answer appears.”

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
text:
Bridge

This is a brief transitional slide. Simply say: “Bridge. We are crossing from user to builder. Everything you have done so far — the queries, the cross-system merges, the charts — all of that was powered by a surprisingly small amount of code. Today you will see it.”
Pause for a beat and advance. Do not linger on this slide.

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
section: You’ve spent a week asking questions and getting answers.
text:
You’ve spent a week asking questions and getting answers.
Now let’s look under the hood.

How does an AI agent turn your English question into SQL, run it, and deliver a polished result? The answer is surprisingly simple — and surprisingly powerful.


This is the main bridge slide. Spend about two minutes here setting up the session arc. Say something like: “You have spent a week asking questions and getting answers. Some of those answers were great. Some were not. But in every case, something was happening behind the scenes — your English question was being translated into SQL, that SQL was being executed against a real database, and the results were being interpreted and formatted for you.”
“Today we are going to demystify that entire process. The answer to ‘how does this work?’ is surprisingly simple at the core. The agent loop that powers the Meridian app — and most BI agents on the market — is about 50 lines of code. We will see those 50 lines today. We will also see why going from 50 lines to a production application takes 3,000 lines, and what all that extra code does. That gap — between prototype and production — is one of the most important things you need to understand as an executive evaluating AI tools.”
Transition: “Let me start with our learning objectives for today.”

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
text:
Learning Objectives

Understand the agent loop as an orchestrated test-and-review cycle
See the 50 lines of code that power the core of the app
Understand where code executes and why that’s an architectural decision


Walk through the three objectives briefly. Say: “Three things we are going to cover. First, the agent loop — the core pattern that every AI data agent uses. It is a test-and-review cycle, not a one-shot generation. Second, we will see the actual code — about 50 lines — that powers the core of the app you have been using. And third, we will talk about where that code runs, because that is an architectural decision with real security implications.”
“These three concepts will give you the vocabulary to have informed conversations with your technical teams and with vendors. You will understand what is simple, what is hard, and where the real engineering effort lives.”
This slide should take about one minute. Then transition: “Let us start with the agent loop.”

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
text:
The Agent Loop

This section divider opens the agent loop demo segment, which runs from roughly minute 10 to minute 30. Say: “The Agent Loop. This is the core pattern.” Then advance.
Before you advance, make sure the Meridian app is open in another window and ready for the demo. You will be showing a cross-system question and walking through what happens behind the scenes — the system prompt, the tool call, the SQL execution, the merge, and the final response.

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
section: What Happens When You Ask a Question?
text:
What Happens When You Ask a Question?



1


System Prompt


Schema description tells the LLM what tables and columns exist




2


Tool Call


LLM writes SQL (or Python) and requests execution




3


Execute & Return


System runs the code and sends results back to the LLM




4


Review & Deliver


LLM checks results, fixes errors or delivers the answer




This is the first conceptual slide of the agent loop segment. This segment should take about 20 minutes total (minutes 10-30), including the live demo. Walk through each step deliberately, pointing to each card as you go.
Say: “When you type a question into the Meridian app — or any AI data agent — four things happen. Step one: the system prompt. Before your question even reaches the LLM, a system prompt has already been loaded that describes the database schema — what tables exist, what columns they have, what the relationships are. Think of this as giving the LLM a map of your data before asking it to navigate.”
“Step two: the tool call. The LLM reads your question, reads the schema, and writes SQL — or sometimes Python — to answer it. But here is the key: it does not just return the SQL to you. It makes a tool call, which is a request to the system to actually execute that code.”
“Step three: execute and return. The orchestration layer — the code that sits between you and the LLM — takes that SQL, runs it against the database, and sends the results back to the LLM. The LLM never touches the database directly. It writes code, and the system executes it.”
“Step four: review and deliver. The LLM looks at the results. If something looks wrong — an empty result set, an error message, numbers that do not make sense — it can try again. It might rewrite the SQL, change a filter, fix a join. If the results look good, it formats them into a natural language response with charts and delivers the answer.”
Transition: “Let me show you this in action. I am going to switch to the Meridian app and ask a cross-system question, but this time I want you to watch what happens behind the scenes.”
DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: Switch to the Meridian app. Ask a cross-system question like “Which customers buy from multiple divisions and what is their total revenue?” Walk through the system prompt, show the SQL that was generated, show the results coming back, show the Python merge step, and show the final formatted response. Narrate each step as it maps to the four cards on this slide.

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
section: The Key Insight: Test and Review
text:
The Key Insight: Test and Review

The agent doesn’t just generate code and hope for the best. It writes code, executes it, reviews the result, and decides whether to deliver or try again.


This is the same pattern a careful analyst follows: write a query, check the output, refine if something looks off, then present the final answer. The LLM automates that entire cycle.


After the demo, come back to this slide to reinforce the key insight. Say: “Here is what I want you to take away from what you just saw. The agent does not just generate code and hope for the best. It writes code, executes it, reviews the result, and decides whether to deliver or try again. That loop — write, execute, review, decide — is the fundamental pattern.”
“Think about how a careful analyst works. They write a query, look at the output, notice something is off, rewrite the query, check again, and only then present the results. The LLM automates that entire cycle. It is not magic — it is a structured test-and-review process.”
“This is also why the agent sometimes takes a few seconds longer than you might expect. It is not slow — it is reviewing its own work. And sometimes it runs through the loop two or three times before delivering an answer, because the first attempt did not look right.”
Ask the room: “Did anyone notice during Sessions 1 or 2 that the agent sometimes seemed to pause or take a moment before answering? That was the review step. It was checking its own work.”

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
text:
The Agent Loop in Pseudocode


agent_loop.py — The Core (~50 Lines)

def agent_loop(question, schema):
    messages = [system_prompt(schema), user_message(question)]

    for attempt in range(3):
        response = llm.chat(messages)
        if response.has_tool_call:
            result = execute(response.tool)
            messages.append(result)        # feed back
        else:
            return response.text           # done


This slide shows the pseudocode for the core agent loop. Walk through it line by line. Say: “Here is the agent loop in pseudocode. This is a simplified version of what the Meridian app runs at its core. Let me walk through it.”
“First, we set up the messages list. It starts with two things: the system prompt, which contains the schema description, and the user’s question. That is all the LLM needs to get started.”
“Then we enter a loop — up to three attempts. On each pass, we send the messages to the LLM and get a response. If the response contains a tool call — meaning the LLM wants to run some SQL or Python — we execute it, take the result, and append it to the messages list. Now the LLM can see what happened. On the next iteration, it will either make another tool call or, if it is satisfied with the results, return a text response.”
“If the response does not contain a tool call, we are done — the LLM is delivering its final answer.”
“That is it. That is the core. The real code has error handling, logging, and some other details, but the logic is this simple. About 50 lines of Python.”
Pause and let that sink in. Then say: “Now, the obvious question is: if it is this simple, why does deployment take months? We will get to that. But first, let me show you the piece that makes this whole thing work — the system prompt.”

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
section: The System Prompt Is the Secret
text:
The System Prompt Is the Secret

This section divider opens the system prompt segment, which runs from roughly minute 30 to minute 45. Say: “The System Prompt Is the Secret.” Then advance.
This is a critical segment because it connects the technical architecture to something executives can directly influence. The system prompt is where business knowledge meets technology, and getting it right is the difference between an agent that works and one that does not.

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
text:
Why the Prompt Matters



Without Join Key Documentation


LLM sees orders and customers tables
No information about shared keys
Agent can’t figure out how to merge
Query fails or returns wrong results




With Join Key Documentation


Prompt says: orders.customer_id links to customers.id
Agent writes correct JOIN
Merges work on first attempt
Accurate, complete answers




This slide sets up the live demo of prompt manipulation. This segment should take about 15 minutes total (minutes 30-45). Say: “The system prompt is the most important piece of the entire system. Let me show you why with a simple example.”
“On the left: without join key documentation. The LLM can see that there are two tables — orders and customers — but it has no information about how they relate. It does not know that orders.customer_id links to customers.id. Without that, it cannot write a correct JOIN, and either the query fails or it returns garbage.”
“On the right: with join key documentation. We add one line to the system prompt — ‘orders.customer_id links to customers.id’ — and suddenly the agent writes correct JOINs on the first attempt. Merges work. Answers are accurate.”
“This is not a hypothetical. Let me show you.”
DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: Switch to the demo environment. Show the system prompt with join key documentation. Ask a question that requires a join. Show it working. Then remove the join key documentation from the prompt. Ask the same question. Show it failing or returning wrong results. Add the documentation back. Show it working again. This is the “break it and fix it” demo described in the course design for minutes 30-45.
After the demo, say: “That single line in the prompt made the difference between a working agent and a broken one. This is why I say the prompt is the secret.”

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
text:
The Prompt Is “Institutional Knowledge”

The system prompt encodes everything the agent needs to know about your data: table names, column meanings, relationships, business rules, and formatting preferences.


Change the prompt, change the behavior. Add a rule like “always filter to active customers” and every query respects it. Remove join documentation and merges break. The prompt is the most important piece of the system.


Say: “The system prompt is institutional knowledge. It is everything your agent needs to know about your data, encoded in plain English. Table names, column meanings, relationships between tables, business rules, formatting preferences — all of it lives in the prompt.”
“And here is the powerful implication: change the prompt, change the behavior. Add a rule that says ‘always filter to active customers’ and every query the agent writes will respect that rule. Add a formatting preference that says ‘always show dollar amounts with commas and two decimal places’ and every response will follow it. Remove the join documentation and merges break.”
“This means that the people who write and maintain the system prompt are effectively the people who control the agent’s behavior. In most organizations, that should be a collaboration between your data team — who knows the schemas — and your business leaders — who know the rules and preferences. It is not a one-time setup. As your data changes, as business rules evolve, the prompt needs to evolve with it.”
Ask the room: “Think about your own organization for a moment. Who has the institutional knowledge about your data — what tables exist, how they relate, what the business rules are? Is that knowledge documented anywhere, or does it live in people’s heads?” Let a few people respond. Then transition: “That documentation problem is exactly what the system prompt forces you to solve. And that brings us to our next question: once the LLM has written the code, where does it actually run?”

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
text:
Where Does the Code Run?

This section divider opens the execution architecture segment, which runs from roughly minute 45 to minute 60. Say: “Where Does the Code Run?” Then advance.
This is a lecture segment, not a demo. The key point is that the LLM generates code but something else has to execute it, and that “something else” is an architectural decision with security implications. This is the first of several deployment decisions executives need to understand — it sets the stage for Session 5.

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
text:
Three Architecture Options



A. Cloud LLM Sandbox


Vendor runs the code (OpenAI, Anthropic)
Simplest to set up
Data leaves your network
Fine for non-sensitive data




B. Your Own Containers


Docker on your servers or cloud
Production answer for sensitive data
You control the execution environment
More infrastructure to manage




C. SQL-Only Mode


No Python execution at all
LLM generates SQL, your DB runs it
Safest for regulated environments
Can’t do in-memory merges or charts




Walk through each option. Say: “The LLM generates SQL and Python. Something has to execute it. There are three main options, and each involves a different tradeoff.”
“Option A: Cloud LLM Sandbox. This is the simplest. The vendor — OpenAI, Anthropic, whoever — runs the code on their servers. You send the query, they execute it, they send back the results. The advantage is simplicity: nothing to set up, nothing to maintain. The disadvantage is that your data leaves your network. The query results pass through the vendor’s servers. For non-sensitive data or for prototyping, this is fine. That is what our Meridian demo uses — synthetic data running through a cloud API.”
“Option B: Your Own Containers. This is the production answer for sensitive data. You run Docker containers on your own servers or in your own cloud environment. The LLM still generates the code, but the execution happens inside your infrastructure. Data never leaves your network. The tradeoff is more infrastructure to manage — you need container orchestration, monitoring, security patching.”
“Option C: SQL-Only Mode. The LLM generates SQL queries, but it never runs Python at all. The SQL goes directly to your database, and the database executes it. This is the safest option for highly regulated environments because the only code that runs is SQL against your own database. The tradeoff is that you lose the ability to do in-memory merges, data transformations, and chart generation. Remember those cross-system merges from Session 2? Those required Python. In SQL-only mode, you cannot do them.”
Ask the room: “Which of these three options do you think would fit your organization? Think about your data sensitivity, your IT infrastructure, and your compliance requirements.” Let a few people share quick thoughts, but do not let this become a long discussion — save the deeper deployment conversation for Session 5.

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
text:
Choosing the Right Option





Cloud Sandbox
Your Containers
SQL-Only




Setup effort
Low
Medium
Low


Data stays on-prem
No
Yes
Yes


Python/charting
Yes
Yes
No


Best for
Prototyping
Production
Regulated





Say: “Here is the summary table. Cloud sandbox is low effort but data leaves your network. Your own containers keep data on-prem and support Python, but require more setup. SQL-only is low effort and keeps data on-prem, but you lose charting and in-memory processing.”
“For most organizations, the answer ends up being Option B for production — your own containers. You prototype with Option A, prove the concept, and then move to containers when you are ready to connect to real data. Some regulated industries — banking, healthcare — might start and stay with Option C.”
“We will revisit this in much more depth in Session 5, when we talk about deployment architecture and data security. For now, the important thing is that you understand this is a decision that needs to be made, and it has real implications for what the agent can and cannot do.”
Transition: “Now let me address the elephant in the room. If the core is only 50 lines, why is this hard? Why does deployment take months? Why do vendors charge what they charge?”

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
text:
The Complexity Iceberg

This section divider opens the complexity iceberg segment, which runs from roughly minute 60 to minute 72. Say: “The Complexity Iceberg.” Then advance.
This is a lecture and discussion segment. The goal is to calibrate expectations: the core is simple, but production is hard. This is the key insight that helps executives evaluate vendors and scope projects realistically.

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
text:
Simple Core, Complex Deployment



~50


Lines of core agent logic




3,000+


Lines in a production app




60x


Growth from prototype to production




Let the numbers speak for themselves for a moment. Say: “Fifty lines of core agent logic. Three thousand lines in a production app. A sixty-times growth factor from prototype to production.”
“The 50 lines are the agent loop we just looked at. The 3,000-plus lines are the actual Meridian app you have been using for the past week. Same core pattern, but wrapped in everything you need to run it reliably, securely, and at scale.”
“This ratio — roughly 60 to 1 — is not unusual. It is typical of most software systems. The core logic of most applications is a small fraction of the total codebase. The rest is infrastructure, security, error handling, and operational tooling.”
“Why does this matter to you as executives? Because when a vendor tells you their AI agent product costs half a million dollars, you need to understand what you are paying for. You are not paying for the 50 lines. You are paying for the other 2,950 lines — and the team that maintains them.”

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
text:
What the Other 2,950 Lines Do


Security & Access

Authentication (who are you?)
Authorization (what can you see?)
Row-level access control
Audit logging



Reliability & Scale

Error handling & retries
Rate limiting
Multi-tenancy
Monitoring & alerting
Failover & backup




The core loop is elegant and simple. The production wrapper is where the engineering effort lives. This is why you buy or build on a platform rather than rolling your own from scratch.


Walk through both columns. Say: “Let me break down what those other 2,950 lines do. On the left: security and access. Authentication — who are you? Authorization — what are you allowed to see? Row-level access control — the VP of Sales should see sales data but not HR compensation data, even though the agent can query both systems. And audit logging — every query, every result, every access, recorded for compliance.”
“On the right: reliability and scale. Error handling and retries — what happens when the database is slow or the LLM API times out? Rate limiting — making sure one heavy user does not consume all the resources. Multi-tenancy — supporting multiple users with different permissions. Monitoring and alerting — knowing when something breaks before your users tell you. Failover and backup — what happens when a server goes down.”
“None of this is glamorous. None of it is AI. It is all plain old software engineering. But it is absolutely essential for production, and it is where 95 percent of the effort goes.”
“This is why the advice for most organizations is: buy or build on a platform. Do not try to roll your own from scratch. The 50-line core is easy. The 3,000-line production wrapper is where the real investment lives.”
Discussion prompt: “For those of you who have IT teams: where does your organization’s capability fit on this spectrum? Could your team build the 50-line core? Could they build the 3,000-line production wrapper? That gap is the build-versus-buy decision we will dig into in Session 5.”
Let a few people respond, then transition: “Now, let me prove to you just how simple the core really is. We are going to build an agent from scratch, right now, live.”

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
text:
Live Build

This section divider opens the live build segment, which runs from roughly minute 72 to minute 85. Say: “Live Build.” Then advance.
Make sure the Linux server is ready. You should have a terminal open with SSH to the server, Claude Code running, and a sample database ready. This is the “your developer could do this” moment. The goal is demystification, not intimidation.
If the live demo environment is not available, switch to the pre-recorded fallback and narrate over it.

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
section: Let’s Build an Agent — Right Now
text:
Let’s Build an Agent — Right Now


Live Demo Plan


Start with a blank file and a sample database
Write the system prompt describing the schema
Add the agent loop (the ~50 lines)
Ask a question and watch it work
Break it (remove join docs) and watch it fail
Fix it and watch it recover



Your developer could do this. The point isn’t that it’s hard — it’s that it’s tractable. The magic is in the prompt design and the execution architecture, not in thousands of lines of code.


This is the live build segment. It should take about 13 minutes (minutes 72-85). Walk through the demo plan on screen before switching to the terminal.
Say: “We are going to build an agent from scratch, right now. I have a blank file and a sample database on the Linux server. I am going to use Claude Code — the same AI coding assistant that is available on your server accounts — and direct it in plain English. Here is the plan.”
Walk through the six steps on screen, then say: “The point of this demo is not to impress you with coding. The point is to show you that the core of these systems is tractable. Your developer could do this. The hard part is not the 50 lines — it is everything else we just talked about. But the core? It is this simple.”
DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: Switch to the terminal. Start with a blank Python file. Tell Claude Code in English to build a simple agent: “Build a Python script that takes a question as input, sends it to Claude with a schema description of our sample database, gets back SQL, runs it, and returns the answer.” Narrate each step as Claude Code writes the code. Once it is working, ask a question that requires a join. Show it working. Then remove the join key documentation from the system prompt. Ask the same question. Show it failing. Add the documentation back. Show it recovering.
Key narration points during the demo: (1) “Notice that we are directing the AI in English, and it is writing the Python for us.” (2) “Here is the system prompt — this is the schema description we talked about.” (3) “Here is the agent loop — the same pattern we saw in the pseudocode.” (4) “Now watch — I am removing the join key documentation. Same question, but now the agent does not know how the tables relate.” (5) “And now I add it back. Same question, correct answer. The prompt is the secret.”
If the live demo fails or the environment is not responsive, say: “Let me switch to the recorded version — same demo, same steps, but pre-recorded so we do not lose time to connectivity issues.” Play the fallback recording and narrate over it.

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
text:
What You Just Saw

A working AI agent, built from scratch in minutes, that can answer business questions by writing and executing SQL against a real database. This pattern is at the core of most BI agents on the market.


After the live build demo, come back to this slide to land the point. Say: “What you just saw was a working AI agent, built from scratch in minutes. It takes a question in English, writes SQL, runs it against a real database, and delivers the answer. That is it. That is the core pattern.”
“And here is the important part: this pattern is at the core of most BI agents on the market. Whether you are looking at Salesforce Agentforce, Microsoft Copilot, or any of the AI-native startups, they all use some version of this loop. The differences are in the production wrapper — the security, the scale, the reliability, the user interface — but the core is the same.”
“So when a vendor shows you a demo, you now know what is happening behind the scenes. And when they quote you a price, you know what you are paying for: not the 50 lines, but the 3,000 lines around them.”
Transition: “Let me recap what we covered today.”

title: Session 3: How Does This Actually Work?
text:
Session 3 Recap



1


Agent Loop


Prompt, generate, execute, review, deliver




2


System Prompt


The institutional knowledge layer that makes it all work




3


Execution


Cloud, containers, or SQL-only — pick your tradeoff




4


Iceberg


50-line core, 3,000-line production app




Walk through the recap quickly. Say: “Four things from today. First, the agent loop: prompt, generate, execute, review, deliver. It is a test-and-review cycle, not a one-shot generation. Second, the system prompt: the institutional knowledge layer that tells the agent about your data, your rules, and your preferences. Change the prompt, change the behavior. Third, execution: the code the LLM generates has to run somewhere, and you have three options — cloud sandbox, your own containers, or SQL-only. Each has tradeoffs. Fourth, the iceberg: 50 lines of core logic, 3,000 lines in production. The core is simple. The production wrapper is where the effort lives.”
“These four concepts give you the vocabulary to evaluate any AI data agent on the market. You understand the pattern, you understand what the prompt does, you understand the deployment options, and you understand the gap between demo and production.”

Are there duplicate records? Inconsistent naming? Missing fields? What pre-work would be needed before an agent could query your systems? This is the reality check on the demo we just saw.”

You just direct it in English, the same way I did in the demo. If you want to get your hands dirty, this is your chance.”

The other 3,000 lines are what production requires.

Knowing the difference is how you evaluate vendors, scope projects, and avoid overpaying.


Let the slide speak for a moment, then close with: “Fifty lines and a good prompt. That is the core. The other 3,000 lines are what production requires. Knowing the difference between these two things is how you evaluate vendors, scope projects, and avoid overpaying.”
“When someone tells you AI is magic, you now know it is not. It is a loop: prompt, generate, execute, review, deliver. When someone tells you AI is easy, you now know the core is easy but production is hard. And when someone quotes you a price, you can ask: what am I paying for? The 50 lines, or the 3,000?”
