title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
From Prototype to Production

“Morgan Stanley deployed GPT-4 to 16,000 financial advisors — choosing a cloud API with zero data retention to satisfy SEC and FINRA requirements. The bottleneck wasn’t the technology. It was the architecture decision.”


— Morgan Stanley case study (provided as pre-reading)


Welcome back everyone. This is Session 5 and today we are shifting gears from using AI agents to deploying them. Let this quote from the Morgan Stanley case sit with you for a moment. Sixteen thousand financial advisors, SEC and FINRA breathing down their neck, and the hardest part was not getting the model to work. It was deciding where the model would run and what data it would be allowed to see. That is exactly what today is about.
You arrived having read three things: the Morgan Stanley case study, the AIMultiple article on cloud versus local LLMs, and the data security primer. So today is not a lecture day. Today is a discussion day. I am going to push you to apply what you read to your own companies, your own regulatory environments, your own data. By the end of the session, you will have a written deployment profile for your capstone use case.
[TIMING: Spend about 2 minutes on this opening slide. Let the quote land, then transition to objectives.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Today’s Learning Objectives


By the end of this session, you will be able to:


Compare LLM deployment options: cloud API, on-premise, and hybrid
Identify data security risks and match them to mitigation strategies
Evaluate build vs. buy vs. extend using a structured framework
Draft a deployment profile for your capstone use case



Let me orient you to where we are headed in the next 85 minutes. Four objectives, all of them practical. By the time you leave today, you will have compared deployment options, matched security approaches to your industry, worked through a build-versus-buy framework, and drafted an actual deployment profile that becomes a section of your capstone.
Notice the arc: the first three objectives give you the frameworks. The fourth objective is where you apply them. Everything we do in the first hour is in service of that deployment profile at the end.
[TIMING: 1 minute. Move quickly to the homework debrief.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Homework Debrief

Alright, let us start with where we left off. You were asked to produce a complete deliverable using the Meridian app — a memo, a comparison, a risk assessment. I want to hear what you built and, more importantly, whether you would trust it enough to send it upstairs.
[TIMING: This section divider is a brief transition. Move immediately to the next slide.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
What Did You Build?



Share Your Deliverable


Walk us through the output from Session 4
What question did it answer?
Who is the audience — and what format did you choose?




The Board-Ready Test


What looks right at first glance?
What would you verify before sending to your board?
Where could the agent have introduced errors?




The gap between “interesting demo” and “decision-grade output” is exactly what today’s session is about.


I would like three or four volunteers to walk us through what they produced. Keep it to about a minute each. Tell us: what question did you ask, who was the intended audience, and what format did you choose — a memo, a dashboard summary, a comparison table?
After each share, I want the rest of the room to apply the board-ready test. Look at it with fresh eyes. What looks polished and credible at first glance? And then the harder question: what would you want to verify before you put your name on it and sent it to your board of directors? Where might the agent have introduced errors — wrong date ranges, double-counted customers, averages that include data they should not?
This is the perfect bridge into today’s topic. The gap between an interesting demo output and something you would stake your reputation on is exactly the gap that deployment decisions, data security, and governance are designed to close. That gap is what the rest of this session addresses.
[TIMING: 8 minutes total for this segment. Call on 3-4 students, about 1.5 minutes each, with brief reactions from the room. If participation is slow, share your own example from the Meridian app to get things started. Transition: “Those verification instincts you just described? They are exactly the right instincts. Now let us talk about the Morgan Stanley case, because they faced the same questions at massive scale.”]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Two Separate Decisions

Before we get into the Morgan Stanley discussion, I want to plant one important idea that connects back to Session 3.
[TIMING: Brief transition slide. Move to the next slide immediately.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Where Does It Run?



Where does YOUR CODE run?


Your application, database connectors, orchestration logic
Typically: your cloud tenant, your VPC, your servers
You control this entirely




Where does THE LLM run?


The large language model that interprets questions and generates SQL
Cloud API, on-premise, or hybrid
This is the deployment decision




Bridge from Session 3: the agent architecture has two halves. Your code and the LLM are separate deployment decisions — and the security implications are different for each.


Remember in Session 3 when we looked under the hood of the agent? We talked about where the code executes — the orchestration logic, the database connectors, the application layer. That code runs on your servers. You control it entirely. But there is a second piece: the large language model itself, the thing that interprets the natural language question and generates SQL. Where does that run? That is a completely separate decision, and it is the deployment decision we are focused on today.
Here is why this distinction matters. You can run your application code on your own servers, inside your own VPC, fully locked down — and still be sending your query results to OpenAI’s servers for interpretation. Or you can keep everything local, but then you are running a smaller, less capable model. These are different tradeoffs with different security implications. The Morgan Stanley case you read is a perfect illustration of how a real company navigated this exact decision.
Let me turn it over to the room. You read the Morgan Stanley case. What struck you?
[TIMING: About 5 minutes for this slide. Spend 2 minutes on the bridge explanation, then open it up to the Morgan Stanley case discussion which continues for the next 7-8 minutes. Key discussion questions: Was cloud API with zero data retention the right call for Morgan Stanley? What would your company have done differently? The a16z survey says 90% of enterprises are testing third-party AI — does that match your experience?]
[DISCUSSION FACILITATION: If the room is quiet, start with a direct question: “Who here works in an industry with regulatory constraints similar to Morgan Stanley’s? What would your compliance team have said about sending data to a cloud API?” Let 3-4 people respond. Push back gently: “But Morgan Stanley did use the cloud API — they just negotiated zero data retention. Is that enough for your industry?”]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Three Deployment Options



Cloud API


OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, etc.
Pay per query (~$0.01–0.05)
No infrastructure to manage
Data leaves your network
Best for: fast start, variable workloads




On-Premise / Private Cloud


Self-hosted open-source models
$50–200K+ upfront investment
Full data control
Requires ML engineering staff
Best for: regulated industries, sensitive data




Hybrid


Route by sensitivity level
Sensitive queries stay on-prem
General queries use cloud API
More complex orchestration
Best for: balanced risk/cost




You read about these three options in the AIMultiple article, so I am not going to lecture through them. Instead, let me add the context the article did not cover and then I want your reactions.
Cloud API is where almost everyone starts. You saw the a16z number: 90% of enterprises are testing third-party AI tools. The economics are compelling — pennies per query, no infrastructure, no ML engineers on staff. The catch is that your data leaves your network. For some of you, that is a non-starter. For others, with the right contractual protections, it is perfectly acceptable.
On-premise is the other end of the spectrum. You are running open-source models like Llama or Mistral on your own GPUs. Full data control, nothing leaves your building. But you are looking at fifty to two hundred thousand dollars in GPU infrastructure, plus you need people who know how to run these models. The quality gap between open-source models and the frontier cloud models is shrinking, but it is still real.
Hybrid is where most sophisticated enterprises are heading. You route queries by sensitivity level. Someone asks about public revenue data? Send it to the cloud API, get the best model. Someone asks about individual compensation or pre-announcement financials? Route that to the on-premise model. The orchestration is more complex, but you get the best of both worlds.
Quick show of hands: based on what you read and what you know about your company, which of these three feels like the right starting point for you? Cloud API? On-premise? Hybrid?
[TIMING: 4 minutes. Use the show of hands to gauge where the room is and transition to the cost slide. If most people say cloud API, note that and say “Let us look at the economics of that choice.”]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Cost Comparison



$0.01–0.05


Per simple query — Cloud API (multi-step queries cost more)




$50–200K+


Upfront — On-premise GPU infrastructure




90%


Of enterprises testing third-party AI tools (a16z 2025 CIO survey)




Cloud API dominates early adoption. But as query volume scales and data sensitivity increases, the economics shift. Morgan Stanley’s choice: cloud API with zero data retention — the LLM never stores the conversation.


Let me put some numbers on this. A simple cloud API query costs one to five cents. That sounds trivial, but do the math: if you have a thousand users running twenty queries a day, that is two hundred to a thousand dollars a day just in API costs. And multi-step queries — the kind we have been running in the Meridian app, where the agent queries multiple systems and merges results — those can cost ten to fifty cents each because they involve multiple LLM calls.
On-premise, you are paying fifty to two hundred thousand dollars upfront for GPU infrastructure, plus ongoing costs for electricity, cooling, and the engineering staff to maintain it. But after that, your marginal cost per query approaches zero.
The crossover point depends on volume and sensitivity. For a pilot with a hundred users, cloud API wins every time. For an enterprise-wide deployment with sensitive data, the math changes. Morgan Stanley made an interesting choice: they went with cloud API but negotiated zero data retention. The LLM processes the query and immediately forgets it. No conversation history is stored on the vendor’s servers. That was how they satisfied SEC and FINRA requirements while still getting access to the best model.
[TIMING: 3 minutes. Transition: “Now let us see the data security problem in action. I want to show you something that should make you uncomfortable.”]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
The Data Security Problem

We have been talking about deployment options in the abstract. Now I want to make the data security problem concrete. You read about the four approaches in the primer. Let me show you why they matter.
[TIMING: Brief transition. Move immediately to the demo slide.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Demo: Data Security in Action



Version 1: No Guardrails


“Show me individual salary data for the Engineering team”
Agent returns names, titles, and exact salaries
Technically correct — but a governance disaster




Version 2: Guardrailed


Same question — blocked or returns aggregates only
“Average salary for Engineering: $142K (n=47)”
The LLM sees the same data; the guardrails control what it returns




The question isn’t whether the AI can access the data. It’s whether it should — and who decides.


[DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: Open the Meridian app. First, with guardrails disabled or in the ungoverned version, type: “Show me individual salary data for the Engineering team.” Let the agent return names, titles, and exact salaries. Let the room see it. Pause for a beat. Then switch to the guardrailed version and ask the same question. Show that it either blocks the query entirely or returns only aggregates: “Average salary for Engineering: $142K, n=47.”]
Look at that first result. The agent did exactly what you asked. The SQL was correct. The data is accurate. And if anyone in your company could run this query, you have a governance disaster on your hands. Individual salary data, by name, returned in seconds to anyone with access to the chat interface.
Now look at the second version. Same question, same underlying data, but the guardrails intercept the query and return only the aggregate. The LLM has the same access to the data — the difference is what the system allows it to return.
This is the core insight from your pre-reading: the question is not whether the AI can access the data. It is whether it should, and who makes that decision. Let me ask the room: which of the four data security approaches from the primer would have prevented that first result?
[TIMING: 8 minutes total for this demo section. Spend 3-4 minutes on the live demo, then 4-5 minutes on the discussion that follows. If the demo has technical issues, describe the scenario verbally using the slide content — the point lands either way.]
[DISCUSSION PROMPT: “Which of the four approaches from the primer — zero data retention, data masking, on-premise models, or role-based filtering — would have prevented that first result?” Answer: role-based query filtering is the direct answer, but zero data retention alone would not have helped here because the problem is not where the data goes, it is who can ask for it.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Four Data Security Approaches









Approach
How It Works
Tradeoff




Zero Data Retention
LLM provider contractually deletes all inputs/outputs
Simplest; still sends data to third party


Data Masking / Anonymization
Strip PII before sending to LLM; re-identify on return
Preserves analytical value; adds latency


On-Premise Models
LLM runs inside your network; data never leaves
Full control; highest cost and complexity


Role-Based Query Filtering
Agent enforces permissions based on user identity
Granular control; requires identity integration





Most production deployments combine two or more of these approaches. Morgan Stanley used zero data retention + role-based access.


You read about these four approaches in the data security primer. Let me add what the primer could not cover: how these approaches combine in practice. Almost no production deployment uses just one of these. Morgan Stanley used zero data retention plus role-based access. A healthcare company might use on-premise models plus data masking plus role-based filtering — three layers.
Let me run a quick poll. Think about your company and the use case you have been developing. Which combination of these approaches would your compliance team require? I want you to think about this concretely, because you are going to need it for the breakout exercise.
Here is what I see most often: companies in regulated industries — finance, healthcare, government — start with on-premise or zero data retention as the baseline, then layer role-based filtering on top. Companies in less regulated industries — tech, retail, manufacturing — often start with cloud API plus zero data retention and add role-based filtering as they scale.
The key insight is that these are not mutually exclusive. They are a menu, and your compliance team will want to see a combination that addresses data in transit, data at rest, and data access. Does anyone have a specific scenario from their company where they can see this playing out? What would your compliance team insist on?
[TIMING: 5 minutes. This completes the data security section. Solicit 2-3 responses from the room. Transition: “Now let us shift to the other big decision. You know where to deploy and how to secure the data. The next question is: do you build this yourself, buy it from a vendor, or extend something you already have?”]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Build vs. Buy

We have covered the “where does it run” question. Now the “who builds it” question. You may have read the DUNNIXER vendor scorecard with your Session 3 homework — if you did, you are ahead. If not, I will walk you through the framework now and we will apply it together.
[TIMING: Brief transition. Move to the next slide.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
The DUNNIXER Framework


Six Dimensions for Evaluating AI Solutions


Data integration — How well does it connect to your existing systems?
User experience — How intuitive is it for non-technical users?
Nuance & accuracy — How well does it handle domain-specific language?
Non-functional requirements — Security, compliance, scalability, uptime
Implementation timeline — Weeks, months, or quarters to production?
Xtensibility — Can you customize prompts, add tools, evolve it?



DUNNIXER gives you a structured way to evaluate any AI solution, whether you are looking at a vendor product or evaluating whether to build internally. Six dimensions, and every one of them matters.
Data integration is often the dealbreaker. If a vendor’s tool cannot connect to your Salesforce and your NetSuite and your legacy CRM, it does not matter how good the AI is. User experience determines adoption — if your sales team finds it easier to email the analyst than to use the AI tool, you have wasted your investment. Nuance and accuracy is about domain specificity: does it understand that “deal size” at your company means closed-won only? Non-functional requirements cover the boring but critical stuff: security certifications, uptime SLAs, compliance attestations. Implementation timeline is straightforward: can you get this live in weeks, or are you looking at a multi-quarter project? And extensibility is about the long game: can you customize the prompts, add new data sources, evolve the tool as your needs change, or are you locked into what the vendor ships?
Let us apply this framework together. We are going to score a specific vendor product — Salesforce Agentforce — on these dimensions. But first, let me show you the four strategic options you are choosing between.
[TIMING: 5 minutes. Walk through each dimension with a brief concrete example. Transition to the four strategic options slide.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Four Strategic Options



Buy from Incumbent


Salesforce, Microsoft, SAP
Lowest risk, fastest start
Limited customization
You’re locked into their roadmap




AI-Native Startup


Purpose-built for AI-first workflows
Highest capability today
Vendor survival risk
May not integrate with legacy




Build Internally


Full control over every layer
Highest customization
Requires ML + engineering talent
Longest time to production




Hybrid / Extend


Buy a platform, customize on top
Example: Salesforce Agentforce
Moderate risk and control
Most common enterprise pattern




Four options, and most of you will land somewhere in the bottom right — the hybrid or extend approach. But let me walk through all four so you understand the tradeoffs.
Buying from your incumbent vendor — Salesforce, Microsoft, SAP — is the lowest risk path. They already have your data, they already have your security certifications, and their AI features are being bolted onto products you already use. The downside is limited customization. You get what they ship, on their timeline. If their AI copilot does not handle your specific workflow, you wait.
AI-native startups are purpose-built for AI-first workflows. They often have the highest capability today because AI is all they do. But there is vendor survival risk. Half of the AI startups that exist today will not exist in three years. And integration with your legacy systems may be nonexistent.
Building internally gives you full control. You saw in Session 3 that the core agent loop is fifty lines of code. But going from fifty lines to production is a different story — authentication, error handling, monitoring, audit logging. You need ML engineering talent, and you are looking at quarters, not weeks.
The hybrid or extend approach is the most common enterprise pattern. You buy a platform — Salesforce Agentforce, Microsoft Copilot Studio, something similar — and then you customize on top. You get the vendor’s infrastructure and security, but you add your own prompts, your own data connections, your own business logic.
Let me ask the room: where does your company typically land on build-versus-buy decisions for technology? Do you tend to buy from incumbents, or does your company have the engineering culture to build?
[TIMING: 5 minutes. Quick discussion to gauge the room’s inclinations before the Agentforce example.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Example: Salesforce Agentforce

What it is: Salesforce’s AI agent platform — extends your existing CRM with conversational AI, pre-built connectors, and guardrails.



Strengths

Already integrated with your Salesforce data
Enterprise security and compliance built in
Low-code customization for business users
Salesforce handles model updates and scaling



Limitations

Only works well within the Salesforce ecosystem
Limited cross-system querying (NetSuite, Workday, etc.)
Pricing tied to Salesforce licensing
You don’t control the underlying model




Agentforce illustrates the extend strategy: buy the platform, customize the prompts and tools, accept the vendor’s guardrails.


Let us score this together using the DUNNIXER framework. I will take us through two or three dimensions as a group exercise.
Data integration: Agentforce gets a high score within the Salesforce ecosystem. If your CRM data is in Salesforce, the connection is native. But how does it score for cross-system queries? If you also need NetSuite financial data or Workday HR data, Agentforce’s reach drops significantly. Think about the Meridian app experience — we were querying across ten systems. Agentforce is strong within one.
Non-functional requirements: Salesforce already has SOC 2 certification, GDPR compliance, and enterprise-grade uptime SLAs. If your compliance team already trusts Salesforce with your data, the incremental trust required for Agentforce is small. That is a real advantage.
Extensibility: You can customize prompts and add tools through their low-code interface. But you do not control the underlying model. If Salesforce switches from one LLM provider to another, you go with them. That is the tradeoff of the extend strategy.
How would you score Agentforce on the remaining dimensions — user experience, nuance and accuracy, and implementation timeline? Anyone want to take a guess based on what you know?
[TIMING: 4 minutes. This is a group exercise. Solicit input from the room on the remaining DUNNIXER dimensions. Transition to the decision matrix.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Decision Matrix











Criterion
Buy (Incumbent)
AI-Native Startup
Build Internally
Hybrid / Extend




Time to value
Weeks
Weeks–months
Quarters
Months


Customization
Low
Medium
High
Medium–High


Data control
Vendor-dependent
Vendor-dependent
Full
Shared


Risk
Lowest
Highest (vendor)
Highest (execution)
Moderate


Cost (Year 1)
$50–200K
$30–150K
$200K–1M+
$100–300K





This matrix is your cheat sheet for the breakout exercise. Let me highlight the tensions.
Time to value versus customization: if you need something live in weeks, you are buying from an incumbent. But you are accepting low customization. If you need high customization, you are building internally, but you are looking at quarters to production.
Data control versus cost: full data control means building internally, which is the most expensive option. If you are willing to share data control with a vendor, you can cut costs significantly.
Risk is interesting because it comes in different flavors. Buying from an incumbent is lowest risk — they are not going anywhere, the product works, your compliance team already knows them. An AI-native startup carries vendor survival risk — will they exist in two years? Building internally carries execution risk — do you have the talent, and can you ship on time?
Look at the cost row. Year one costs range from thirty thousand dollars for a startup tool to over a million for an internal build. These are real numbers that your CFO will want to see. The hybrid approach typically lands in the hundred to three hundred thousand range, which is why it is the most common enterprise pattern.
You are going to use this matrix in the breakout. Keep it in mind.
[TIMING: 3 minutes. Walk through the key tensions quickly. This is reference material, not deep lecture.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
What If You’re Not Ready?

Not every company is ready for an AI agent deployment. If you don’t have documented schemas, API access, or data catalogs, the honest answer may be: invest in data readiness first.



Signs You Should Wait


No one can describe your schema in plain English
API access requires a 6-month procurement process
Your data has no owner or steward
Compliance has never been asked about AI access to production data



The 60–90 days of pre-work from Session 2 applies here. Data readiness is the prerequisite that no vendor demo will mention.


I want to be honest with you about something that no vendor will tell you. Not every company is ready for this. If you are sitting there thinking “my company does not even have documented schemas” or “it would take six months just to get API access to our production databases,” then the honest answer for your capstone might be: the first phase of your AI strategy is data readiness, not AI deployment.
Remember Session 2, when we talked about the sixty to ninety days of pre-work? Cataloging your data, documenting your schemas, provisioning API access, classifying PII? That pre-work is not optional. It is the prerequisite. And if your company has not done it, that is actually a valuable finding for your capstone. Your deployment profile might say: “Phase 1 is data readiness, Phase 2 is a pilot deployment.”
There is no shame in that. In fact, a capstone that honestly says “we are not ready yet, and here is the six-month roadmap to get ready” is more credible than one that pretends you can deploy an AI agent next quarter when you cannot even describe your schema.
How many of you recognize your company in this list? Be honest. Show of hands. That is valuable self-awareness, and it should shape your deployment profile.
[TIMING: 3 minutes. Transition: “Alright, you have the frameworks. Now it is time to apply them. Let us move into the breakout.”]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Breakout: Your Deployment Profile

This is the core exercise of the session. You have spent the last forty-five minutes absorbing deployment options, data security approaches, and the build-versus-buy framework. Now you apply all of it to your own use case.
[TIMING: Brief transition. Move to the breakout instructions.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Breakout Exercise


For Your Use Case (20 minutes)


Deployment: Which option — cloud API, on-premise, or hybrid? Why?
Security: Which combination of data security approaches fits your industry?
Build vs. Buy: Where does your organization land on the decision matrix?
Constraints: What regulatory, budget, or talent constraints shape the decision?



Each team presents a 2-minute summary. Be specific: name the deployment option, the security approach, and the build/buy decision — with your reasoning.


Here is how this works. You are going into breakout rooms of three to four people. You have twenty minutes. Each person should take about four minutes to present their use case and get feedback from the group. Use the four questions on the screen as your structure.
Be specific. I do not want to hear “we would probably use the cloud.” I want to hear “we would use cloud API with zero data retention because we are in manufacturing and our data is not regulated, but we would add role-based filtering because our HR data should not be visible to operations managers.” That level of specificity.
When you come back, each group presents a two-minute summary. I want you to report two things: what constraints were common across your group members, and where did industry differences lead to different answers? For example, did the healthcare person in your group land on a completely different deployment option than the retail person? Why?
Use the DUNNIXER dimensions to justify your build-versus-buy choice. If you say “we would buy from our incumbent,” tell me which DUNNIXER dimensions drove that decision. Was it data integration? Implementation timeline? Non-functional requirements?
I will drop into breakout rooms to listen in and answer questions. You have twenty minutes starting now.
[TIMING: 20 minutes in breakout rooms (minutes 58-72 of the session, with ~6 minutes for group reports when they return). Circulate among rooms. Listen for interesting tensions between group members. When groups return, call on 3-4 groups for 2-minute summaries. Highlight patterns: “I noticed three groups independently chose hybrid deployment — why do you think that is?”]
[AFTER BREAKOUT: When groups return, facilitate a 6-minute report-out. Ask each group: “What was the biggest disagreement in your group, and how did you resolve it?” This surfaces the real tensions executives face.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Individual Work: Capstone Deployment Profile


Write Your Deployment Profile (15 minutes)


Use case: One sentence describing your AI data agent
Deployment: Cloud API / On-prem / Hybrid — and why
Security: Which approaches and why
Build / Buy / Extend: Your recommendation with rationale
Key risk: The single biggest risk and your mitigation plan



This becomes a section of your capstone. Draft it now while the frameworks are fresh.


Now I want you to take everything from the discussion, the breakout, and the frameworks, and write it down individually. This is not a group exercise. This is your deployment profile for your capstone use case. You have fifteen minutes.
Five bullet points on the screen. Work through them in order. One sentence for the use case. Then your deployment choice with a rationale — not just “cloud API” but “cloud API because our data sensitivity is moderate and we lack on-premise GPU infrastructure.” Then your security approach — which combination of the four approaches and why. Then your build-versus-buy recommendation with a DUNNIXER-informed rationale. And finally, the single biggest risk and how you would mitigate it.
This becomes a section of your final capstone strategy document. You are not starting from scratch in Session 8 — you are building the capstone piece by piece. This is the deployment piece.
I will circulate and offer feedback. If you are stuck on any dimension, raise your hand. The most common place people get stuck is the risk and mitigation — so think about it from your compliance team’s perspective. What would keep them up at night?
[TIMING: 13 minutes of individual work (minutes 72-85). Circulate the room. Look for students who are stuck and offer targeted advice. Common issues: students who cannot choose between cloud and hybrid (push them to commit and justify), students whose “risk” is too generic (“data quality” — push them to be specific about what kind of data quality issue). At the 12-minute mark, give a 1-minute warning.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Before Next Session


Homework


For your identified use case: what governance requirements would your compliance team impose? Write down your top 3 concerns.
Think about who in your organization would need to approve an AI agent deployment — and what objections they’d raise.
Complete your deployment profile draft if you haven’t already.



Wednesday’s session: Can You Trust It? — we stress-test AI agents, learn the maker/checker framework, and design governance guardrails.


Three homework items, and they are all about governance — which is exactly where we are headed on Wednesday. First, put yourself in your compliance team’s shoes. For the use case you just drafted a deployment profile for, what governance requirements would they impose? Write down your top three concerns. These might be things like audit trails, access controls, data residency requirements, or regulatory-specific mandates.
Second, think about the approval chain. Who in your organization would need to sign off on an AI agent deployment? Your CIO? Your chief compliance officer? Your CISO? What objections would each of them raise? Write those down, because on Wednesday we are going to design governance frameworks that address exactly those objections.
Third, if you did not finish your deployment profile, complete it before Wednesday. You will need it.
Wednesday’s session is called “Can You Trust It?” and it is going to be a very different experience. We are going to deliberately break the Meridian app. We are going to show you three failures that look like successes, teach you a verification framework, and then turn you loose to red-team the agent yourselves. Come ready to be skeptical.
[TIMING: 3 minutes. Be clear and concise on the homework. Transition to the closing.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
text:
Key Takeaway

Let me leave you with one thought.
[TIMING: Brief transition. Move to the final slide.]

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
section: The hardest part isn’t the model.
text: The hardest part isn’t the model.

title: Session 5: What Does Deployment Look Like?
section: It’s the decisions around the model.
text:
It’s the decisions around the model.

Where it runs, what it sees, and who controls it. Get those wrong, and the demo that impressed your CEO becomes the breach that ends your quarter.


The hardest part is not the model. It is the decisions around the model. Where it runs, what it sees, and who controls it. You worked through all three of those decisions today. You have a deployment option, a data security approach, and a build-versus-buy recommendation. Those are the decisions that separate a compelling demo from a production deployment.
Get those decisions wrong, and the demo that impressed your CEO becomes the breach that ends your quarter. Get them right, and you have the foundation for an AI capability that scales across your organization.
See you Wednesday. Come ready to break things.
[TIMING: 1 minute. End on a strong note. Total session time: approximately 85 minutes plus 5 minutes of buffer.]

title: Session 7: Document AI — Answers from Your Own Files
text:
Last Session: Trust and Verification

Welcome back everyone. Last session we spent a full ninety minutes on trust and verification — red-teaming agents, seeing how they fail, and building governance frameworks. That was all about structured data, databases, and SQL. Today we pivot to a different kind of company knowledge entirely. Over the past six sessions, you have learned to query databases, build reporting pipelines, and evaluate reliability. All of that was structured data — rows and columns. But think about where the bulk of your company’s knowledge actually lives. It is not in databases. It is in policies, contracts, board minutes, emails, and slide decks buried in SharePoint folders nobody can search. Today we add a second capability — document AI — and by the end of the session, you will have used it hands-on.
Before we dive in, let me set the stage with a case study you read for homework. The Atherton case shows exactly what can go wrong when document AI is deployed without the right guardrails. Let’s debrief that case first, and then everything we do today will be informed by those failure modes.
[TIMING: This section divider is your opening. Spend about 1 minute here, then advance to the Atherton debrief. Total for bridge plus debrief is 10 minutes.]

title: Session 7: Document AI — Answers from Your Own Files
text:
Bridge + Atherton Case Debrief



What Atherton Deployed (composite case)


Document AI across compliance policies, vendor contracts, and HR handbooks
Employees could ask questions in plain English and get cited answers
Replaced a manual process of searching SharePoint and emailing Legal




What Went Wrong


Chunking failures — long contract clauses split mid-sentence, wrong answers returned
Access control gaps — junior staff saw board-level compensation data
Stale documents — system answered from a superseded policy version




What would you have done differently? We’ll return to each of these failure modes today.


So Atherton Financial is a composite case, but every one of these failures comes from real deployments. Let me walk through the left card first. Atherton built a document AI system that covered compliance policies, vendor contracts, and HR handbooks. The promise was compelling — employees could ask questions in plain English and get answers with citations, replacing a manual process where people searched SharePoint, gave up, and emailed the Legal department. Sound familiar? I bet most of you have some version of this at your companies.
Now look at the right card — what went wrong. Three distinct failure modes. First, chunking failures. The system split long contract clauses mid-sentence. So when someone asked about indemnification terms, the system returned half the clause and gave an incomplete, actually dangerous answer. Second, access control gaps. Junior staff could suddenly see board-level compensation data because the RAG system did not inherit the document permissions from the file server. And third, stale documents. A policy was updated, but the old version was still in the index, so the system confidently delivered outdated information.
Here is my question for you, and I want three or four quick reactions. If you had been advising Atherton before they deployed, what would you have done differently? Think about what we covered in Session 6 on governance. Drop your thoughts in the chat or unmute.
[TIMING: Spend about 2 minutes presenting the case, then 3-4 minutes on discussion. Aim to get 3-4 responses. Listen for themes around testing, access controls, and document management. When you hear good points, connect them forward: “Great — we are going to see exactly that failure mode in our hands-on today.” Wrap the debrief by minute 10.]
Transition: Good. Those three failure modes — chunking, access control, and freshness — are the exact issues we will dig into today. But first, let me show you what document AI looks like when it works well.

title: Session 7: Document AI — Answers from Your Own Files
text:
Today’s Learning Objectives


By the end of this session, you will be able to:


Explain how AI answers questions from company documents — with citations
Distinguish database AI from document AI and know when to use each
Experience RAG hands-on and understand key deployment decisions



Three objectives for today. First, you will understand the mechanics of how AI answers questions grounded in company documents, complete with citations pointing back to specific pages and sections. This is not the AI making things up — it is retrieving actual passages from your files and synthesizing an answer. Second, you will be able to clearly distinguish database AI, which is everything we have done in Sessions 1 through 6, from document AI, which is today’s focus. Knowing when to use each — and when you need both — is a critical skill. Third, you will experience this hands-on. You are going to query a real document collection and see for yourselves where it excels and where it breaks down.
[TIMING: About 30 seconds on this slide. It is a signpost, not a lecture. Move on quickly.]

title: Session 7: Document AI — Answers from Your Own Files
section: Two Kinds of AI for Business
text:
Two Kinds of AI for Business



Database AI (Sessions 1–6)


Queries structured data in rows and columns
Writes SQL, runs it, returns results
Best for: numbers, trends, KPIs, comparisons
Source of truth: your transactional systems




Document AI (Today)


Queries unstructured text in PDFs, Word docs, policies
Retrieves relevant passages, synthesizes an answer with citations
Best for: policies, contracts, procedures, meeting notes
Source of truth: your document repositories




Most real business questions need both. “What’s our contractual commitment to GE, and how does their actual spending compare?” requires document AI for the contract and database AI for the spending data.


Let me frame what we are adding today. On the left is everything you already know — database AI. It queries structured data in rows and columns, writes SQL, runs it, and returns numbers, charts, and narratives. That is Sessions 1 through 6 in a nutshell. On the right is document AI, which is today’s focus. Instead of structured data, it works with unstructured text — PDFs, Word documents, policies, contracts, meeting notes. Instead of writing SQL, it retrieves relevant passages from those documents and synthesizes an answer with citations.
The key distinction is the source of truth. Database AI pulls from your transactional systems — your CRM, your ERP, your financial systems. Document AI pulls from your document repositories — SharePoint, shared drives, contract management systems. The data types are fundamentally different, and the retrieval mechanisms are fundamentally different. But here is the important point at the bottom of the slide. Most real business questions need both. Read that example in the explainer: “What is our contractual commitment to GE, and how does their actual spending compare?” The contract terms live in a PDF. The actual spending lives in a database. You need document AI for the first part and database AI for the second. We are going to see exactly that kind of combined question in our hands-on today.
[TIMING: About 2 minutes on this slide. This completes the bridge segment. Transition to the demo next.]
Transition: Alright, let me show you document AI in action. I am going to switch to the Meridian app and start asking questions of a real document collection.

title: Session 7: Document AI — Answers from Your Own Files

text:
Demo: Document Q&A with Meridian Corp

[DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: Switch to the Meridian Corp app in your browser. You should have the document Q&A interface ready. This is a 12-minute demo from minute 10 to minute 22. Have your fallback recording cued up in case the app or API is down.]
We are going to query the same Meridian Corp environment you have been using all course, but now we are going to ask it about documents instead of databases. Meridian has a pre-loaded document collection that mirrors what you would find at any mid-size company — employee handbooks, vendor contracts, compliance policies, board minutes. Let me show you the collection first, and then we will start asking questions.
[TIMING: Spend about 30 seconds on this divider slide, then advance to show the document collections.]

title: Session 7: Document AI — Answers from Your Own Files
text:
Meridian’s Document Collections



HR & Compliance


Employee handbook
Travel & expense policy
Code of conduct
Safety procedures




Legal & Contracts


Vendor agreements
Master service agreements
Non-disclosure agreements
SLA commitments




Governance


Board meeting minutes
Compliance audit reports
Risk assessments
Strategic plans




These are the same kinds of documents trapped in SharePoint folders, shared drives, and email attachments at every company.


Before I start the live demo, let me orient you to what is in the document collection. Three categories. On the left, HR and Compliance — employee handbook, travel and expense policy, code of conduct, safety procedures. In the middle, Legal and Contracts — vendor agreements, master service agreements, NDAs, SLA commitments. On the right, Governance — board meeting minutes, compliance audit reports, risk assessments, strategic plans.
Now, think about this for a moment. These are the same kinds of documents trapped in SharePoint folders, shared drives, and email attachments at every single company. How do people find information in these documents today? They search SharePoint, maybe get lucky with a keyword match, probably give up and email someone in Legal or HR or Compliance. That email sits in a queue for two days. That is the status quo we are about to replace.
[DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: Now switch to the live app. Ask these questions in sequence, pausing to point out the citations after each answer:

“What is Meridian’s policy on remote work for the Energy Division?” — Point out the citation with page number and quoted passage. Emphasize that this is not the AI making something up; it is retrieving and quoting from the actual document.
“Which vendor contracts are up for renewal in Q2?” — Show how it pulls from multiple contracts and synthesizes a list. Note the citations for each contract mentioned.
“What did the board approve regarding the Safety Division expansion?” — Show it pulling from board minutes. Point out that this information would normally require someone to read through pages of minutes.
The combined question: “What is our contractual commitment to GE, and how does their actual spending compare?” — This is the key demo moment. Show that RAG retrieves the contract terms while the database agent pulls the spending data. Two different retrieval paths, one synthesized answer.

If the app is down, switch to the pre-recorded fallback and narrate over it.]
Let me ask the first question. “What is Meridian’s policy on remote work for the Energy Division?” Watch the answer — and pay attention to the citations.
[TIMING: This demo runs from minute 10 to minute 22. Spend about 2 minutes per question, with time for brief reactions between questions. The GE combined question should be the climax — spend an extra minute showing how the two systems work together. If you are running long, you can skip question 3 about board minutes.]
Transition: So you have seen document AI in action — retrieval, synthesis, citations. Now let me explain how it actually works under the hood.

title: Session 7: Document AI — Answers from Your Own Files
text:
How RAG Works



1


Chunk


Documents split into passages (paragraphs, sections, or clauses)




2


Embed


Each chunk converted to a numerical vector by an embedding model




3


Store


Vectors stored in a vector database for fast similarity search




4


Query


User’s question embedded the same way; top matching chunks retrieved




5


Generate


LLM receives question + retrieved chunks and produces a cited answer




RAG = Retrieval-Augmented Generation. The LLM only sees what the retrieval step finds. If retrieval fails, the answer fails — no matter how smart the model is.


RAG stands for Retrieval-Augmented Generation. Let me walk you through the five steps. You do not need to memorize these, but understanding the pipeline will help you evaluate vendors and diagnose problems.
Step 1: Chunk. Your documents are split into passages — paragraphs, sections, or clauses depending on the document type. This is where the Atherton chunking failure came from. If you split a contract clause in the middle of a sentence, you have already lost. Step 2: Embed. Each chunk is converted into a numerical vector by an embedding model. Think of this as translating text into a position in a high-dimensional space where similar meanings end up near each other. “Remote work policy” and “work from home guidelines” would end up close together even though they use different words. Step 3: Store. Those vectors go into a specialized database — a vector database — that is optimized for finding similar vectors quickly. Step 4: Query. When a user asks a question, that question goes through the same embedding process, and the vector database finds the stored chunks that are closest in meaning to the question. Step 5: Generate. The LLM receives the user’s question plus the top matching chunks as context, and it produces an answer using only that retrieved context, with citations pointing back to the source documents.
Here is the critical insight, and I want you to underline this in your mental model. Read the explainer at the bottom. The LLM only sees what the retrieval step finds. If retrieval fails, the answer fails — no matter how smart the model is. You could have the most advanced language model in the world, and if step 4 hands it the wrong chunks, the answer will be wrong. RAG quality is retrieval quality. That is why chunking strategy, which we will discuss later, is the single highest-leverage decision in any document AI deployment.
[TIMING: Spend about 5 minutes on this slide, walking through each step. This is the conceptual heart of the session. Make sure people understand the pipeline before moving to hands-on. You should be at about minute 27.]

title: Session 7: Document AI — Answers from Your Own Files
text:
The Key Insight

Let me make this point as starkly as possible. This is the single most important thing to understand about document AI.
[TIMING: Pause on this divider for just a beat, then advance to the statement slide. The dramatic pause is intentional.]

title: Session 7: Document AI — Answers from Your Own Files
section: The LLM doesn’t read your documents.
text: The LLM doesn’t read your documents.

title: Session 7: Document AI — Answers from Your Own Files
section: It reads whatever the retrieval system hands it.
text:
It reads whatever the retrieval system hands it.

RAG quality is retrieval quality. If the retrieval step fails, the answer fails — no matter how smart the model is.


I want you to internalize this. The LLM does not read your documents. It reads whatever the retrieval system hands it. This means that when you are evaluating a document AI vendor, or scoping a build, the question is not “which LLM are you using?” The question is “how good is your retrieval?” How do you chunk? How do you embed? How do you handle documents that span multiple topics? How do you handle updates? The retrieval pipeline is where the quality lives or dies.
Think back to Atherton. Their LLM was fine. Their retrieval was broken. Contract clauses split mid-sentence, so the model got half a clause and produced a confident, dangerously incomplete answer. The model did exactly what it was supposed to do — it answered based on the context it was given. The problem was upstream, in the chunking.
[TIMING: About 1-2 minutes on this slide. Let it sink in. You should be at about minute 30. Transition to the hands-on rounds next.]
Transition: Now it is your turn. You are going to query the Meridian document collection yourselves. We will do three rounds, each one more challenging than the last.

title: Session 7: Document AI — Answers from Your Own Files
text:
Hands-On Round 1: Simple Retrieval


Round 1 — Single-Document Questions


“What is Meridian’s policy on remote work?”
“What are the expense approval thresholds?”
“How many vacation days do employees get after 5 years?”
Notice the citations — each answer points back to a specific document and section



Start simple. Ask questions that live in a single document. Pay attention to where the system says the answer came from.


Alright, Round 1. Open the Meridian app and switch to the document Q&A mode. I have three starter questions on the screen, but feel free to ask your own as well. “What is Meridian’s policy on remote work?” “What are the expense approval thresholds?” “How many vacation days do employees get after 5 years?”
These are intentionally simple — each answer lives in a single document. The goal of this round is to get comfortable with the interface and, more importantly, to start noticing the citations. Every answer should point back to a specific document and section. When you get your answer, check the citation. Does it actually say what the AI claims it says? This is the verification habit we talked about in Session 6, applied to documents instead of data.
You have about five minutes. Go ahead and start asking questions. I will be watching the chat for anything interesting.
[TIMING: 5 minutes of hands-on time, from about minute 32 to minute 37. Monitor the chat. If someone finds an interesting result or a surprising answer, call it out to the group. Common things to watch for: questions where the citation is spot-on, questions where the phrasing matters (e.g., “PTO” vs. “vacation days”), and questions that return multiple citations from the same document.]
After about 4 minutes: Alright, let me pull you back. Quick show of hands or reactions in chat — did everyone get cited answers? Did anyone find a case where the citation did not quite match the answer? Good. Let’s make it harder.

title: Session 7: Document AI — Answers from Your Own Files
text:
Hands-On Round 2: Cross-Document


Round 2 — Questions Spanning Multiple Documents


“What are our obligations under the GE contract, and are there any compliance risks flagged in the latest audit?”
“Does our travel policy align with what we committed to in the Apex Industries MSA?”
“What did the board say about vendor consolidation, and which contracts are up for renewal?”



These questions require the system to retrieve from multiple documents and synthesize. Watch for gaps where it finds one source but misses the other.


Round 2 gets more interesting. These questions deliberately span multiple documents. Look at the first one: “What are our obligations under the GE contract, and are there any compliance risks flagged in the latest audit?” That requires the system to retrieve from the GE vendor agreement and from the compliance audit report, then synthesize both into a coherent answer.
The second question asks whether the travel policy aligns with what was committed in a vendor MSA. The third asks about board discussion on vendor consolidation cross-referenced with actual contract renewal dates. These are the kinds of questions that, in a normal company, would require someone to pull up three or four documents and read through them manually. That is a multi-hour task. Here, it should take seconds.
But watch for gaps. The system might find one source and miss the other. It might retrieve the GE contract terms but fail to find the relevant section of the audit report. When that happens, you are seeing a retrieval miss in real time — and that is exactly the failure mode we discussed. Pay attention to which citations appear and which are absent.
You have about seven minutes. Try these questions and also try your own cross-document questions.
[TIMING: 7 minutes of hands-on time, from about minute 37 to minute 44. This round often generates more discussion because students start seeing gaps and inconsistencies. Watch for: answers that only cite one document when two are needed, answers where the synthesis is good but shallow, and the inevitable question about why the system missed something obvious.]
After about 6 minutes: Let me hear some reactions. Did anyone find a case where the system found one document but missed the other? That is a classic retrieval miss — the answer exists in the collection, but the retrieval step did not surface it. This is why we said RAG quality is retrieval quality. One more round, and this one is the most powerful.

title: Session 7: Document AI — Answers from Your Own Files
text:
Hands-On Round 3: Cross-System


Round 3 — Document AI + Database AI Together


“What’s our contractual commitment to GE, and how does their actual spending compare?”
“The board minutes mention expanding the Energy Division — what do the sales numbers show?”
“Our vendor agreement with Apex caps annual spend at $2M — are we on track to exceed it?”



This is where it gets powerful. The system pulls the contract language from document AI and the actual numbers from database queries. Two systems, one answer.


This is where it all comes together. Round 3 combines document AI with database AI — the same database querying you have been doing since Session 1. Look at the first question: “What is our contractual commitment to GE, and how does their actual spending compare?” The contractual commitment lives in a PDF — that is document AI. The actual spending lives in the transactional database — that is database AI. The system needs to use both retrieval paths and synthesize the results.
The second question references board minutes about expanding the Energy Division and asks what the sales numbers show. The third checks whether actual vendor spending is on track to exceed a contractual cap. In each case, you need the document for the qualitative context and the database for the quantitative evidence. This is the full vision — structured and unstructured company knowledge, accessible through one interface, answering questions that previously would have required multiple people, multiple systems, and multiple days.
You have about eight minutes. Try these and see how the system handles the combination. Pay attention to whether both sources are cited — the document and the database query.
[TIMING: 8 minutes of hands-on time, from about minute 44 to minute 52. This is the climax of the hands-on portion. Students are often impressed by the combined answers. Watch for: moments where the combination works beautifully (call these out), moments where one path succeeds and the other fails, and students who start asking their own creative cross-system questions. Encourage experimentation.]
After about 7 minutes: Let me bring everyone back. This is the vision we have been building toward across all eight sessions. Database AI for the numbers. Document AI for the context. Combined into answers that would have taken days to assemble manually. But — and this is important — it is not magic. Let me show you where it breaks down.

title: Session 7: Document AI — Answers from Your Own Files
text:
When RAG Fails



Retrieval Misses


Question phrased differently than source text
Relevant chunk not in top results
Result: correct answer exists but system can’t find it




Hallucinated Citations


LLM generates plausible-sounding references
Citation points to wrong section or nonexistent page
Result: answer looks authoritative but is wrong




Stale Documents


Policy was updated but old version still indexed
System answers from superseded document
Result: confidently delivers outdated information




Live demo: Watch what happens when a contract clause is split across two chunks. The system retrieves half the clause and gives a dangerously incomplete answer.


Now let me show you the failure modes. Three categories, and each one maps back to the Atherton case we discussed at the opening.
First, retrieval misses. The answer exists in the document collection, but the retrieval step does not find it. This happens when the question is phrased differently than the source text, or when the relevant chunk does not rank high enough to make the top results. The answer is there — the system just cannot find it. Some of you may have seen this in Round 2 when the system found one document but missed the other.
Second, hallucinated citations. This is the most dangerous failure mode. The LLM generates a plausible-sounding reference — it cites a specific page number, quotes what looks like a passage — but when you go check, that passage does not actually say what the AI claims, or it does not exist at all. The answer looks authoritative, which makes it harder to catch. This is why we said in Session 6: always check the cited passage, not just the summary.
Third, stale documents. A policy was updated, but the old version is still in the index. The system answers from the superseded document and confidently delivers outdated information. This was one of Atherton’s failures, and it is incredibly common in real deployments because document management is one of those things nobody wants to own.
[DEMO INSTRUCTIONS: Now run the chunking failure demo. Ask a question where you know a contract clause was split across two chunks. Show the partial answer. Explain that the system retrieved half the clause and gave a dangerously incomplete answer. This is the Atherton failure mode made visible. If the live demo is not cooperating, describe the failure mode verbally and reference what students may have seen in their own hands-on rounds.]
Let me show you this live. I am going to ask a question where I know the relevant contract clause was split across two chunks during ingestion. Watch what happens.
[TIMING: About 5 minutes total on this slide, from minute 52 to about minute 57. Spend 2 minutes on the three failure modes, then 3 minutes on the live chunking failure demo. The demo is the most impactful part — students seeing a confident but incomplete answer in real time drives the point home.]
Transition: So chunking failures, hallucinated citations, and stale documents. These are the risks. But there is one more constraint you need to understand before we talk about deployment.

title: Session 7: Document AI — Answers from Your Own Files
text: Context Window Limits

title: Session 7: Document AI — Answers from Your Own Files
section: Even the best models have a ceiling
text:
Even the best models have a ceiling

If your question requires context from 50 pages of contracts, the model can only see what fits in its context window. Retrieval quality matters because you can’t just dump everything in. Chunking strategy determines what the model gets to see.


One more technical constraint that is important for your mental model. Even the best language models have a context window — a limit on how much text they can process at once. Today’s frontier models can handle very long contexts, but there is still a ceiling. If your question requires context from fifty pages of contracts, the model cannot see all fifty pages at once. The retrieval step has to select which passages to include.
This is why you cannot just dump all your documents into the LLM and skip the retrieval step. Some people ask, “Why not just send the entire contract to the model?” For a single short document, you can. But for a collection of hundreds of documents, or even for a single hundred-page contract, you need retrieval to select the relevant passages. Chunking strategy determines what the model gets to see, which means chunking strategy determines the quality of the answer. We keep coming back to the same point: retrieval quality is everything.
[TIMING: About 2 minutes on this slide. You should be at about minute 59. Quick conceptual point, then move to deployment.]
Transition: So now you understand how RAG works, you have used it hands-on, and you have seen where it fails. Let’s talk about what it takes to deploy this in your organization.

title: Session 7: Document AI — Answers from Your Own Files
text:
Deploying RAG: Key Decisions



1


Document Ingestion


PDF parsing, OCR for scans, handling tables and images




2


Chunking Strategy


By section for policies, by clause for contracts, by paragraph for memos




3


Freshness


Re-index on update, version tracking, retire superseded docs




4


Access Control


Document-level permissions must carry over — who can see what




Chunking is the single highest-leverage decision. Bad chunking = bad retrieval = bad answers, regardless of model quality.


The deployment framework from Session 5 — cloud versus on-premise, build versus buy — still applies here, so we are not going to repeat those general principles. What is new for document AI are these four specific decisions.
First, document ingestion. How do documents get into the system? You need PDF parsing that handles different formats. You need OCR for scanned documents. You need to handle tables and images, which are notoriously difficult for text extraction. If your company has contracts that are scanned PDFs of faxes from 2003, that is an ingestion challenge you need to plan for.
Second, chunking strategy, and I have been hammering this point because it is the single highest-leverage decision. The right chunking strategy depends on the document type. Policies should be chunked by section. Contracts should be chunked by clause — you never want to split a clause across two chunks, which is exactly what happened at Atherton. Memos and meeting notes should be chunked by paragraph or topic. One size does not fit all, and the vendor that tells you otherwise is selling you something.
Third, freshness. When a policy is updated, the old version must be replaced, not duplicated. You need re-indexing on update, version tracking so you know which version the system is answering from, and a process to retire superseded documents. Atherton’s stale document problem came from having no freshness management at all. The old policy and the new policy were both in the index, and the system returned whichever the retrieval step happened to find first.
Fourth, access control. The RAG system must enforce the same document permissions as your file server. If board minutes are restricted to senior leadership, the RAG system cannot show those passages to everyone. If HR policies are scoped by division, the system needs to respect those boundaries. Atherton’s access control gap — junior staff seeing board-level compensation data — is the cautionary tale here.
[TIMING: About 5 minutes on this slide, from about minute 62 to minute 67. Walk through each decision with enough detail that people understand the implications. Connect each one back to the Atherton case. This is lecture plus Q&A, so take a question or two if hands go up.]

title: Session 7: Document AI — Answers from Your Own Files
text:
Build vs. Buy










Turnkey Platforms
Custom Build




Examples
Glean, Guru, Microsoft Copilot for M365
LangChain + vector DB + your infra


Setup time
Days to weeks
Weeks to months


Customization
Limited — works with standard doc formats
Full control over chunking, retrieval, prompts


Access control
Built-in (inherits from source system)
You build it


Cost model
Per-user subscription
Infrastructure + engineering time


Best for
Broad knowledge base across standard tools
Domain-specific docs, regulatory, proprietary formats





Most companies start with a turnkey platform for general knowledge and build custom RAG only where the domain requires it (legal, compliance, engineering specs).


For document AI specifically, the build-versus-buy landscape looks like this. On the left, turnkey platforms — Glean, Guru, Microsoft Copilot for M365. These handle ingestion and permissions out of the box. They connect to your existing document sources — SharePoint, Google Drive, Confluence — and they inherit the access controls from those systems. Setup time is days to weeks, and the cost model is typically per-user subscription. The tradeoff is limited customization. You get their chunking strategy, their retrieval approach, their prompts.
On the right, custom builds using tools like LangChain with a vector database on your own infrastructure. This gives you full control over chunking, retrieval, and prompts. You can implement domain-specific chunking — by clause for contracts, by regulation for compliance documents. But you build the access control yourself, and the setup time is weeks to months rather than days.
The practical pattern I see most often is what the explainer describes. Companies start with a turnkey platform for general knowledge — the employee handbook, standard policies, IT documentation. Then they build custom RAG only where the domain demands it — legal contracts with specific clause structures, regulatory filings with complex cross-references, engineering specifications with tables and diagrams that standard chunking cannot handle.
For your capstone strategies, think about where your company falls on this spectrum. Do you have standard documents that a turnkey platform could handle? Or do you have domain-specific documents that require custom chunking and retrieval?
[TIMING: About 5 minutes on this slide, from about minute 67 to minute 72. Walk through the comparison table row by row. Take a question or two. Students often ask about Microsoft Copilot specifically — have a brief answer ready about its strengths (tight Office 365 integration, built-in access controls) and limitations (less customization, dependent on Microsoft ecosystem).]
Transition: Alright, you have seen document AI work, you understand the pipeline, you know the failure modes and deployment decisions. Now I want you to apply this to your own companies. Time for a breakout.

[BREAKOUT INSTRUCTIONS: This is a 12-minute breakout from minute 75 to minute 87. Groups of 3-4. Assign breakout rooms in Zoom. Visit each room briefly during the breakout to listen and prompt discussion.]
Alright, breakout time. I am going to put you into groups of three or four. You have twelve minutes for this exercise. Here is what I want you to do.
First, the Identify card on the left. Each person in the group should name two or three document collections at your company that would benefit from AI-powered Q&A. Think broadly — policies, contracts, procedures, meeting notes, customer communications, regulatory filings, engineering specs. And for each one, talk about where people currently go to find this information. Is it a SharePoint search? An email to someone? A phone call? A folder that only one person knows about?
Second, the Evaluate card on the right. For your top candidates, assess three things. How often are these documents updated? This tells you about the freshness challenge. Who needs access and who should not have it? This tells you about the access control challenge. And what is the cost of a wrong answer from stale or incomplete documents? If the cost is low — someone gets the wrong lunch menu — maybe it is fine. If the cost is high — someone gives a wrong answer about indemnification terms to a client — that is a deployment risk you need to plan for.
When you come back, each group should be ready to report: your top candidate document collection and the biggest deployment obstacle you foresee. I will be visiting your rooms to listen in. Off you go.
[TIMING: 12 minutes in breakout rooms. Visit 2-3 rooms during the breakout. Listen for patterns — the same types of documents come up repeatedly (vendor contracts, internal policies, client communications). When groups return, spend about 3-5 minutes on report-backs. Call on 3-4 groups. Listen for: which document types came up most often, what access control challenges were identified, and whether groups leaned toward turnkey or custom solutions. Connect the themes back to the deployment framework.]
Welcome back. Let me hear from a few groups. What was your top candidate document collection, and what is the biggest deployment obstacle? Let me start with Group 1.
[After report-backs:] Great. I am hearing some common patterns. Vendor contracts and internal policies came up in almost every group. Access control is a universal concern. And most of you are thinking turnkey first, custom only where the domain demands it. Those are exactly the right instincts. Keep these ideas warm — they may feed directly into your capstone strategy for next session.

Homework — Capstone

Monday’s final session: Your AI Strategy — you present, we synthesize, and you leave with a concrete plan.


Let me walk you through what is due for our final session on Monday. Three things.
First, finalize your strategy document. One to two pages using the capstone template I shared with the Session 6 homework. It has eight sections: the problem, the solution, the deployment approach, a 90-day pilot plan, success metrics, risks and mitigations, the ask — what you need in terms of budget and headcount — and the Day 1 action. Every section matters, but let me highlight three that trip people up. The pilot should be scoped tightly — one use case, one to three systems, a clear success metric. The success metrics should be measurable, not vague. “Reduce forecast time from 3 days to 2 hours” is strong. “Improve efficiency” is not. And the Day 1 action should be something that actually happens on Day 1 — a specific person, a specific task, a specific date. Not “we should explore options.”
Second, prepare a five-minute presentation. On Monday, you will present to your breakout group first. Each group will vote on the strongest strategy, and those winning strategies will present to the full class. Everyone presents; five advance to the plenary.
Third, use the evaluation rubric to self-check before you finalize. The rubric was included with the template. Run your strategy through each dimension and ask yourself honestly: would I fund this?
If you want to go deeper on the technical side, the optional Demos 6 and 7 show building a RAG system from scratch and combining it with a database agent. They are linked in the homework materials.
[TIMING: About 2 minutes on this slide. Be clear about expectations for Monday. You should be wrapping up at about minute 88.]

Key Takeaway

title: Session 7: Document AI — Answers from Your Own Files
section: Your most valuable knowledge is buried in documents nobody can search.
text:
Your most valuable knowledge is buried in documents nobody can search.

RAG doesn’t replace your experts. It makes their expertise accessible to everyone who needs it.


Your most valuable knowledge is buried in documents nobody can search. The policies that guide decisions, the contracts that define obligations, the board minutes that record strategy — all of it sitting in folders that require you to know exactly where to look and exactly what to search for. Document AI changes that. It does not replace your experts. Your legal team still needs to interpret contract terms. Your compliance team still needs to make judgment calls. What document AI does is make their expertise accessible to everyone who needs it, on demand, with citations.
Think about what that means for your organization. The new hire who needs to understand the travel policy does not email HR and wait two days. The procurement manager who needs to check vendor contract terms does not call Legal and wait a week. The board member who wants to know what was decided six months ago does not search through email threads. The knowledge is there. It just becomes findable.
That is the promise. The Atherton case shows the risks. Today you have experienced both. On Monday, you bring it all together in your capstone strategy. See you then.
[TIMING: About 1-2 minutes to close. End at minute 90. You are done.]
